tests/config/split/unit.tests.tst.description: This folder holds unit-test fixture data for the configuration “split” mechanism at the record level. The fixtures define multiple DNS record types (A, AAAA, CNAME, DNAME, MX, NS, PTR, TXT, SPF, SRV, NAPTR, SSHFP, URLFWD and related structured types) with TTL and typed fields, including escaping/serialization-sensitive values. Several fixtures also encode octodns-specific inclusion/exclusion/ignored semantics to validate that the split loader and downstream planning logic interpret scope directives consistently.
key_behaviors: Loads per-record fixtures from YAML and expects deterministic parsing into typed record structures (including multi-value and structured fields).
Applies octodns inclusion/exclusion/ignored directives to decide whether records participate in the resulting zone for a scoped test run.
Correctly handles nested/dotted record names (e.g., mapping 'www.sub' style names) within split configuration organization.
Preserves formatting-sensitive values (notably TXT escaping and NAPTR/SPF/URLFWD structured strings) across parsing and comparisons.

insights.title: Inclusion/exclusion/ignored directives affect fixture participation
insights.problem: Fixtures are intentionally marked to represent different selection states (excluded, ignored, included for a specific scope).
insights.root_cause: Split configuration logic must interpret octodns.* metadata and apply it during record aggregation for a test scope.
insights.solution: When updating parsing or split-loader behavior, ensure directive evaluation is applied consistently across all record types and that scope matching remains stable.

insights.title: Record-type specific structures must round-trip faithfully
insights.problem: Several fixtures use structured fields (MX exchanges, SRV targets, NAPTR entries, URLFWD match/masking) and multi-value arrays.
insights.root_cause: Typed record models require correct mapping from YAML fields to internal representation, including ordering and data types.
insights.solution: Changes to YAML parsing, record model schemas, or serialization must keep field names, ordering semantics (where relevant), and array handling compatible with these fixtures.

insights.title: TXT/SPF-like strings and NAPTR regexp/replacement are comparison-sensitive
insights.problem: Fixtures include escape sequences and regex-like strings where incorrect escaping alters semantic meaning.
insights.root_cause: YAML parsing and later serialization/comparison may apply or remove escaping depending on implementation details.
insights.solution: Any updates affecting string handling must preserve intended literal values exactly as represented by fixtures, including backslashes and quoting behavior.

insights.title: Nested hostnames must map deterministically to split paths
insights.problem: Fixtures include record names with dots (e.g., 'www.sub') that must resolve to the correct split configuration location.
insights.root_cause: Split configuration relies on a naming-to-path convention that can be sensitive to delimiter handling.
insights.solution: Maintain stable mapping rules for dotted names so that records land in the correct aggregated zone structure during tests.

insights.title: A single “unit.tests.yaml” fixture acts as a baseline set keyed by an empty-string YAML key
insights.problem: One fixture uses a YAML construct keyed by an empty string with merge/anchor-like syntax to provide a suite of record definitions.
insights.root_cause: Test harness likely expects a particular YAML shape to produce multiple record definitions under one domain context.
insights.solution: Avoid altering YAML loading behavior that would interpret that structure differently (e.g., handling of empty-string keys, merge keys, anchors).

key_files.why: Acts as the baseline multi-record YAML fixture defining the test dataset and exercising YAML shape/merge/anchor behavior.
Provides SRV record fixture with weighted/priority/target fields to validate structured parsing for service-target records.
Contains TXT strings with escape-sensitive content to validate correct quoting/escaping through split parsing and comparisons.
Exercises octodns.included scoping behavior and ensures selective inclusion works as expected in split aggregation.
Validates nested/dotted hostname mapping ('www.sub') to split configuration paths and record naming logic.

construct_relationships: The directory’s fixtures collectively define expected inputs for the split configuration loader, which aggregates typed record models into a Zone/plan used by unit tests.
Fixtures labeled with octodns.included, octodns.excluded, or octodns.ignored are used to verify that record selection filtering occurs before/while building the resulting zone for a given test scope.
Record fixtures with multi-value 'values' or array-like structured entries validate that the underlying parsing layer populates internal list fields in a way compatible with later comparisons.
notable_relationships: Downstream consumers are unit tests (outside this folder) that likely load these YAML fixtures via a split-config harness and compare parsed/serialized results; changes in fixture structure or interpretation can break those comparisons.

These fixtures depend on shared record-type schemas and parsing rules used elsewhere in the project (e.g., models for MX/SRV/NAPTR/TXT/SPF/URLFWD), so any schema/parsing adjustments must remain compatible with the exact YAML field structures used here.
recommendations: When editing any split-config loading, YAML parsing, record-type schema, or string escaping/serialization logic, re-check that all fixture YAML shapes (including empty-string keyed baseline dataset constructs) are still interpreted identically.

Preserve directive semantics for octodns.included, octodns.excluded, and octodns.ignored across all record types; selection behavior must remain stable for the unit-test scopes these fixtures represent.
Maintain deterministic handling for nested/dotted record names so 'www.sub' and similar names continue mapping to the same split paths and resulting record names.
Ensure that escaping-sensitive TXT/SPF and NAPTR string fields remain byte-for-byte equivalent in parsed/serialized forms used by the unit tests.

tests/config/split/subzone.unit.tests.ts.description: This folder contains unit test fixtures for validating DNS configuration splitting at the subzone level. The YAML files define minimal A-record entries for different hostnames, which tests likely load to verify merge/split behavior and consistent parsing across multiple files. Because these are fixtures, their exact structure, naming, and values are part of the contract that the test harness relies on.
key_behaviors: Provides deterministic YAML inputs representing individual DNS A records intended for subzone split scenarios
Supports tests that combine or compare records across multiple fixture files to verify correct splitting/merging semantics
Validates that record parsing and host identification remain consistent when records are distributed across subzone-related inputs

insights.title: Fixtures represent isolated A records with specific host/value pairs
insights.problem: Tests likely depend on the exact hostname keys and IP values to assert correct placement and grouping in subzone split logic.
insights.root_cause: Fixture YAML encodes the minimal record identity (host) and payload (A value), which is used for comparisons.
insights.solution: When editing or extending fixtures, preserve the existing record identity semantics (hostnames '2', '12', 'test' and corresponding IPs) so assertions remain valid.

insights.title: Fixture format must match what the test loader expects
insights.problem: If the YAML schema differs (field names, nesting, record type representation), tests may fail at load time or produce different in-memory models.
insights.root_cause: The folder is designed as loader-compatible test inputs rather than arbitrary data.
insights.solution: Maintain schema compatibility with the config loading logic used by the tests (including any required top-level keys and record fields).

insights.title: Multiple fixtures are intended to be merged/compared as part of subzone logic
insights.problem: Changing fixture content can alter which subzone bucket a record belongs to, leading to mismatched expected results.
insights.root_cause: The fixture set implies a scenario where records are distributed across multiple files and then processed together.
insights.solution: Any modifications must ensure the scenario still represents the same subzone distribution/invariant the tests are asserting.

key_files.why: Primary fixture representing an A record for host '2' used for subzone split/merge assertions.
Fixture representing an A record for host '12', exercising host naming and grouping behavior in subzone logic.
Fixture representing an A record for host 'test', supporting multi-file consistent record loading checks.

construct_relationships: Each YAML fixture file provides an A-record construct (hostname identity + A value) that is consumed by shared subzone/split unit test logic outside this folder
The set of fixtures collectively forms the input corpus for subzone record file splitting/merging comparisons
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: test harness/config loader in the unit tests likely reads all YAML files under this directory to build an expected/actual model for subzone splitting
Downstream dependency: DNS model/record handling assertions rely on these fixtures to reflect specific record types and host-to-subzone mapping behavior
recommendations: When editing fixture YAML, ensure the schema and required top-level structure remain compatible with the test loader for split/subzone unit tests
Preserve record identity semantics (hostnames and A values) unless the corresponding expected assertions are updated to match the new fixture behavior
Keep the directory-level intent intact: these fixtures are likely processed together as an input set for merge/split validation, so changes can have cross-file effects

tests/config/hybrid/two.test..description: This folder contains hybrid configuration test fixtures focused on TXT records and their placement/targeting behavior when the owner name may be empty (root label) and when record data comes from split zone files. These fixtures matter because they exercise naming edge cases and the hybrid system’s ability to correctly interpret/route record definitions into the expected zone-file contexts. Changes to fixture structure or semantics can directly impact the determinism and correctness of hybrid planning/apply tests.
key_behaviors: Represents TXT record configuration where the owner label can be the empty string (root label).
Defines hybrid records that must be interpreted relative to zone/file targeting rules (including how root-label records are handled).
Simulates sourcing record data from split zone files to validate hybrid configuration behavior and record resolution.
Uses sentinel/example values to verify that the correct record ends up in the intended processing path.

insights.title: Root label represented via empty-string key
insights.problem: Hybrid naming/zone-file targeting may treat root-label (owner name empty) differently from standard FQDN keys, potentially causing misplacement or mismatched lookups.
insights.root_cause: Fixture encodes the owner name as an empty string key in YAML, which tests code paths sensitive to label formatting and normalization.
insights.solution: When editing fixture YAML, preserve the exact key representation for the root label (empty-string key) and ensure the record type/value remain consistent so assertions still validate the intended targeting behavior.

insights.category: Hybrid sourcing from split zone files
insights.title: Split-zone-file driven record definition
insights.problem: Hybrid configuration behavior can diverge when record data originates from split zone files, including how record names are resolved and merged.
insights.root_cause: Fixture encodes a specific record name ('split-zone-file') with a sentinel value to force the test to traverse the split zone-file resolution path.
insights.solution: Maintain the fixture’s record name and sentinel value to ensure downstream assertions correlate to the split-zone-file path rather than a default or aggregated source.

insights.title: Fixture-driven expected outputs
insights.problem: Downstream tests likely depend on these fixtures to produce stable plan/apply outcomes; even minor structural changes can alter produced records or selection logic.
insights.root_cause: These YAML fixtures are intended to be exact inputs for the hybrid configuration test suite.
insights.solution: Keep file naming, YAML structure, and record definitions compatible with whatever harness loads them; any change can require corresponding updates to test expectations.

key_files.why: Primary fixture for hybrid handling of a root-level TXT record using an empty-string owner label key.
Fixture that exercises hybrid behavior when sourcing TXT record data from split zone files using a sentinel value.

construct_relationships: Both YAML fixtures define TXT records that are intended to drive different resolution/targeting paths in the hybrid configuration test harness.
The empty-string owner label fixture is expected to validate name normalization/target selection logic for root-label records.
The split-zone-file fixture is expected to validate merge/resolution logic when record data is sourced from split zone files.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: The hybrid test harness (outside this folder) likely loads all YAML fixtures under tests/config/hybrid/two.test. and uses them as configuration inputs for the hybrid planning/application pipeline.

Downstream dependency: Assertions elsewhere in the test suite likely rely on the exact presence, naming, and values of the TXT records from these fixtures to validate correct zone-file targeting and record resolution.
recommendations: When editing these YAML fixtures, preserve the exact record type (TXT), record names/keys (including the empty-string key for the root label), and sentinel values, as downstream assertions likely depend on them.
Do not change file names or paths within this subtree without updating any harness/test discovery logic that may glob or reference them.
Ensure YAML syntax and key quoting for the empty-string owner label remains valid and identical in meaning, since label normalization is part of what the tests are validating.
Run the relevant hybrid test suite to confirm that any fixture changes still produce the expected plan/apply outcomes deterministically.

tests/config/split/unordered.tst.description: This folder contains test fixtures for the “split” configuration test suite, specifically targeting behavior where record loading/splitting order is expected to be irrelevant. The YAML files model small DNS record fragments used to validate that unordered input still produces deterministic planning/output. Because these are fixture-driven tests, changes to fixture structure, naming, or record semantics can directly alter test expectations and coverage boundaries.
key_behaviors: Provides minimal YAML DNS record fragments to simulate unordered loading scenarios
Ensures that splitting logic treats record order deterministically for planning/outputs
Supports multi-fixture composition where separate record keys (e.g., abc, xyz) contribute to a combined expected result

insights.title: Record identity and ordering invariance
insights.problem: Unordered split loading tests rely on fixture records having stable identifiers (e.g., owner name and record type) so the planning result is independent of YAML ordering or merge order.
insights.root_cause: If fixture keys/record fields change (name/type/value structure), the computed diff/plan may change even if the underlying ordering invariance logic still works.
insights.solution: Preserve record identity fields and their intended values when editing fixtures; validate that any change does not alter the expected merged/planned record set.

insights.title: Fixture comments imply ordering assumptions
insights.problem: The fixtures contain comments indicating ordering expectations, suggesting the test suite may interpret ordering-related behavior (or assert about ordering stability) based on fixture layout.
insights.root_cause: Altering comment placement or structural ordering in YAML can affect the developer intent or, if parsing is sensitive, the actual computed order used in the test.
insights.solution: When modifying fixtures, consider how YAML ordering (and any parser behavior regarding order) impacts the specific unordered-split test assertions.

insights.title: Minimal records for targeted behavior
insights.problem: The fixtures are intentionally small (single A records for distinct keys), so they target a narrow behavior surface: unordered loading rather than full-zone complexity.
insights.root_cause: Expanding fixture scope can unintentionally change the test’s coverage and make failures ambiguous (ordering vs. other configuration aspects).
insights.solution: Keep fixtures focused on the ordering invariance scenario; ensure any additions/edits remain within the same behavior boundary.

key_files.why: Provides the A record fixture (abc with 9.9.9.9) used to validate unordered split behavior around record identity and merging.
Provides the second minimal A record fixture (xyz) that, together with abc.yaml, supports deterministic combined results under unordered loading.

construct_relationships: Each YAML fixture defines DNS record data that is consumed by the split/unordered test harness to construct a combined configuration input
The plan/output under test is expected to be invariant with respect to the relative ordering of these fixture inputs
notable_relationships: Downstream: These fixtures are likely referenced by a tests/config/split/unordered.tst runner that aggregates multiple YAML snippets into a single scenario.
Upstream: The fixtures must match the schema expected by the YAML config loader for A records (field names/types/structure) so they can be parsed into internal record objects consistently.
recommendations: When editing or reformatting YAML fixtures, preserve record identity fields (owner/name and record type) and intended values so they continue to assert the same unordered-loading invariance behavior.
Avoid changes that could affect YAML parsing order or merge semantics (e.g., rearranging record structures in ways that alter loader behavior) for this specific unordered test case.
Run the split/unordered test(s) that consume this folder to confirm the fixtures still produce the same deterministic planning/output expected by the test harness.

tests/config/include/subdir.description: This folder contains configuration include fixtures used by the test suite to validate YAML !include behavior across nested directories. Its contents matter because include resolution must correctly locate files relative to the including YAML and parse scalar payloads without altering content. Changes to this fixture can affect test expectations around nested include semantics.
key_behaviors: Provides a nested YAML file meant to be resolved by an include mechanism from another YAML context
Ensures scalar content (a single string value) is parsed and surfaced correctly through the include pipeline
Validates that directory depth does not break include path resolution

insights.title: Scalar preservation through nested includes
insights.problem: Including YAML content that is only a scalar can fail if parsing or include expansion treats the node type incorrectly (e.g., quoting/typing/path handling).
insights.root_cause: Include resolution and YAML loading logic may special-case mappings/sequences but not scalars, or may mishandle file-relative paths at deeper levels.
insights.solution: Maintain the fixture structure and expected scalar value precisely so nested include resolution can be verified end-to-end.

key_files.why: Directly referenced as a nested YAML include target to test scalar parsing and relative path resolution.

construct_relationships: The test include mechanism in the broader test suite likely references this YAML file to produce an expanded value in a parent YAML document.
notable_relationships: Downstream dependency: other tests under tests/config/include likely rely on this exact file path and the scalar payload it contains.
recommendations: Preserve the file path and relative directory depth semantics used by nested !include resolution tests.
Do not change the payload value or YAML shape unless corresponding test expectations are updated, since scalar content is part of the verification signal.
Ensure any modifications remain compatible with the test suite’s YAML loader expectations (scalar typing and quoting).

tests/config/include.description: This folder contains YAML fixture files used by the test suite to validate a custom YAML inclusion mechanism (via a !include tag) and related merge behavior. It matters because correct include resolution, loader behavior, and error handling are typically implemented in shared YAML loading utilities, and these fixtures encode the expected semantics. The fixtures cover arrays, dictionaries, empty documents, nested/relative includes, YAML merge keys, and failure cases for missing or unsupported include forms.
key_behaviors: Resolves !include targets for both scalars and container types (lists and mappings) into the including YAML document
Supports nested/recursive include resolution where the included path is derived relative to the including document’s location
Preserves loader/class consistency across included documents, affecting key ordering and merge semantics
Implements YAML merge key (<<) interactions when merge inputs originate from included mappings, including correct precedence of local overrides
Validates and enforces supported include input forms, rejecting unsupported !include usages
Emits deterministic failures when include targets are missing, including for cases where multiple includes include at least one missing file
Handles empty included content without breaking downstream merge/defaulting behavior

insights.title: Relative path and nesting correctness
insights.problem: Including YAML from subdirectories requires computing the correct include root relative to the including document, not the process working directory.
insights.root_cause: Path resolution is commonly tied to the current file context in YAML loader implementations; incorrect anchoring breaks nested includes.
insights.solution: When editing loader/include logic elsewhere, ensure relative resolution stays anchored to the including YAML file and supports directory depth without changing parsed values.

insights.title: Included document loader parity affects merge results
insights.problem: Out-of-order keys in an included fixture are used to verify that included YAML is parsed using the same loader/class as the parent, impacting how merge keys and mappings behave.
insights.root_cause: Using a different loader for included files can alter parsing behaviors such as ordering, tag handling, or merge behavior.
insights.solution: Maintain consistent loader/tag handling across parent and included documents to preserve deterministic merge precedence and output structure.

insights.title: YAML merge key precedence with included dicts
insights.problem: Merge semantics must correctly combine included mappings and local overrides when the merge key (<<) points to included content.
insights.root_cause: Incorrect handling of merge evaluation order or timing relative to tag resolution can change which keys win.
insights.solution: Ensure merge evaluation occurs with the included mapping fully resolved using the correct precedence rules.

insights.title: Mixed container inclusion
insights.problem: Fixtures validate that multiple !include items can be combined into a single resulting container (e.g., an array containing elements from dict and array includes).
insights.root_cause: Naive include implementations may assume uniform include types or may wrap/unpack incorrectly.
insights.solution: Confirm include resolution returns content in the exact shape expected by the YAML context (list elements vs nested containers).

insights.title: Deterministic errors for missing and unsupported includes
insights.problem: Tests expect failures both when targets do not exist and when an unsupported !include form is used.
insights.root_cause: Error paths may vary by exception type/message, and validation may not be applied early enough to prevent confusing downstream errors.
insights.solution: When changing include/loader validation, preserve consistent failure conditions for missing files and unsupported include forms as asserted by tests.

insights.title: Empty included YAML stability
insights.problem: Empty included content must not produce unexpected nulls or structural changes that break merging/defaulting logic.
insights.root_cause: Empty YAML documents can parse to None/empty dict depending on loader settings, which affects later merge behavior.
insights.solution: Keep the semantics for empty documents stable so downstream code sees the expected empty structure.

key_files.why: Top-level fixture that exercises !include across multiple types and nested paths, defining the end-to-end expected resolved configuration.
Fixture specifically targeting YAML merge key (<<) behavior when merge inputs come from included dicts.
Fixture validating recursive/nested include resolution and correct relative path handling.
Failure-oriented fixture ensuring missing include targets produce expected error behavior (including when bundled with other valid includes).
Fixture designed to validate that included YAML is parsed with the same loader/class as the parent, affecting deterministic behavior such as ordering/merging.

construct_relationships: main.yaml establishes a composite configuration by including array.yaml, dict.yaml, empty.yaml, nested.yaml, and subdir content via !include, so resolution correctness depends on consistent loader/tag handling across all included targets.
include-array-with-dict.yaml and include-dict-with-array.yaml define how the output shape must be composed when included sources are heterogeneous (list within dict or dict within list).
nested.yaml and subdir/value.yaml are linked to validate recursive include resolution using relative paths derived from the including file’s location.
merge.yaml links dict.yaml into a mapping via the YAML merge key (<<), so include resolution timing and merge precedence must align with expected key override rules.
dict_too.yaml is intended to be included under a scenario that verifies loader parity/order-sensitive behavior when included documents define mapping content used in merges or final assembly.
notable_relationships: These fixtures are likely coupled to a custom YAML loader/tag implementation (supporting !include and YAML merge evaluation). Changes to YAML parsing configuration, custom constructors, or include path resolution logic outside this folder may require maintaining the semantics these fixtures assert.
The subdir/fixtures connect to nested include tests; altering how the loader determines a base directory for relative includes can break these tests even if the main include mechanism still works.
recommendations: Preserve include semantics for arrays and dicts exactly as represented by these fixtures (including how included content is inserted vs wrapped).
Maintain relative-path anchoring behavior for nested includes to avoid test failures tied to directory depth and context.
Ensure the include mechanism uses the same YAML loader/tag handling behavior for included documents as it does for the parent document to keep deterministic merge/ordering behavior stable.
Keep error handling deterministic for missing targets and unsupported include forms, including when multiple includes are combined.
Maintain stable parsing behavior for empty YAML documents so downstream merge/defaulting sees the expected empty structure.

tests/config/dynamic-arpa.description: This folder contains YAML fixtures used by the test suite to validate dynamic reverse DNS (ARPA) behavior, specifically A/AAAA and PTR-related generation and matching logic. The fixtures appear intentionally sparse/empty for certain reverse domains, indicating tests verify correct “no output” behavior as well as deterministic output for known dynamic zones. This matters because these fixtures likely drive expectations about how the system interprets reverse-domain patterns and when it should emit DNS records.
key_behaviors: Provides deterministic input zone content for PTR generation and reverse-domain matching tests
Validates that dynamic ARPA scenarios correctly produce no records when inputs/domains should not yield PTRs
Supports multiple reverse-domain families (in-addr.arpa for IPv4 and ip6.arpa for IPv6) with separate expected outcomes
Ensures tests remain stable by using small, explicit fixture data for record derivation

insights.title: Empty YAML fixtures encode 'no records expected'
insights.problem: Some fixtures have empty content, which means test expectations likely rely on the absence of generated records rather than specific record contents.
insights.root_cause: Fixture emptiness is used as an assertion mechanism for dynamic ARPA generation behavior.
insights.solution: When modifying or adding tests/fixtures in this folder, preserve the intended meaning of empty vs. non-empty YAML so expectation logic continues to align.

insights.title: Unit fixture contains minimal zone-relevant record entries
insights.problem: PTR generation and dynamic ARPA matching depend on specific record inputs; changing fixture data can alter which reverse domains should match and produce outputs.
insights.root_cause: Dynamic reverse mapping is sensitive to AAAA/A record values and zone matching rules.
insights.solution: Treat the non-empty fixture as a stable, tightly-scoped dataset; any edits must be consistent with the mapping assumptions used by the test harness.

insights.title: Separate IPv4 and IPv6 reverse fixtures imply distinct code paths
insights.problem: IPv4 in-addr.arpa and IPv6 ip6.arpa reverse domains often follow different formatting and nibble logic; a single change may break one family while the other still passes.
insights.root_cause: The fixture set distinguishes IPv4 vs IPv6 scenarios and expected outputs.
insights.solution: When adjusting related logic, ensure both reverse-domain families continue to satisfy their corresponding fixture expectations (including 'no output' cases).

key_files.why: Represents an IPv4 in-addr.arpa dynamic scenario where the expected result appears to be empty, making it critical for validating 'no records' behavior.
Represents an IPv6 ip6.arpa dynamic scenario where the expected result appears to be empty, critical for validating IPv6 reverse formatting/matching yields no PTRs when appropriate.
Non-empty fixture providing deterministic AAAA/A inputs for PTR generation and reverse-domain matching logic used by unit tests.

construct_relationships: The empty in-addr.arpa YAML fixture is used as an expected outcome for the IPv4 dynamic ARPA/PTR mapping logic.
The empty ip6.arpa YAML fixture is used as an expected outcome for the IPv6 dynamic ARPA/PTR mapping logic.
The non-empty unit.tests.yaml fixture provides the source record inputs that the reverse mapping logic uses to determine whether the reverse-domain fixtures should have corresponding PTR outputs.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency on whatever test runner/harness loads YAML fixtures under tests/config/dynamic-arpa and maps them to computed DNS outputs for dynamic ARPA/PTR generation.

Downstream dependency on reverse-domain parsing and PTR emission logic that must interpret AAAA/A inputs from unit.tests.yaml consistently with the reverse-domain labels represented by the empty fixtures.
recommendations: Preserve the meaning of empty YAML fixtures: they likely represent strict expectations that no DNS records should be produced for those domains.
Maintain the correspondence between reverse-domain filenames (domain labels) and the mapping logic used by the tests; filename/domain label changes can invalidate expectations.
When editing unit.tests.yaml or any fixture data, account for sensitivity to AAAA/A values and how they influence reverse-domain construction and matching.
Ensure any changes that affect IPv4 vs IPv6 reverse parsing are validated against both the in-addr.arpa and ip6.arpa fixture expectations.

tests/config/hybrid.description: This folder contains YAML fixtures used by the test suite to validate “hybrid” configuration behavior around TXT records and their mapping into zone-file contexts. The fixtures focus on edge cases involving record owner names (including the root label as an empty string) and on how record data is interpreted when it originates from flat zone files versus split zone files. Because these fixtures encode expected semantics for hybrid naming/targeting, they strongly constrain how the underlying config/planning logic must behave.
key_behaviors: Loads and interprets hybrid configuration fixtures that define TXT records from different source modalities (flat zone file vs split zone file).
Handles record owner naming edge cases where the owner label may be an empty string (root label) and ensures correct targeting into the expected zone/file context.
Distinguishes how record definitions are routed depending on whether the hybrid source is a flat zone file or a split zone file.

insights.title: Root-label TXT placement is exercised explicitly
insights.problem: Hybrid behavior can mis-handle records whose owner name is empty (root label), leading to incorrect label resolution or incorrect zone/file targeting.
insights.root_cause: Owner-name parsing/normalization and downstream targeting logic can treat empty-string labels as missing/invalid rather than a valid root label.
insights.solution: Ensure that any changes affecting hybrid record interpretation preserve deterministic root-label handling as encoded by these fixtures.

insights.title: Flat-zone-file vs split-zone-file TXT sourcing must remain distinguishable
insights.problem: Records may be resolved using the wrong context (e.g., flat-zone vs split-zone) if source modality flags or structure are changed.
insights.root_cause: Hybrid configuration routing may depend on configuration shape/keys indicating the underlying data source type.
insights.solution: When editing hybrid-related parsing/config logic, keep modality-driven routing consistent with what these fixtures represent.

insights.title: Fixtures embed sentinel/specific values to assert correct resolution
insights.problem: If resolution or ordering changes, tests can become nondeterministic or assert unexpected record placement/content.
insights.root_cause: Plan/apply stages may rely on record targeting, name normalization, or stable mapping between fixture fields and generated outputs.
insights.solution: Avoid altering interpretation rules (especially naming/targeting) in ways that change the derived zone-file context implied by sentinel values.

key_files.why: Primary hybrid fixture covering TXT record sourcing from a flat-zone-file context, validating flat-zone TXT processing behavior.
Targets a root-label TXT record using an empty-string key, exercising naming/owner-label edge cases for hybrid targeting.
Covers TXT record sourcing from a split zone file, validating hybrid behavior when record data comes from split-zone contexts.

construct_relationships: Hybrid test fixtures in this subtree represent parallel scenarios that the test harness uses to drive the same hybrid planning/apply pipeline with different source/modality inputs.
Root-label TXT fixture (empty owner label) is expected to flow through the same record interpretation logic as other fixtures but yield different targeting due to owner-label semantics.
notable_relationships: These fixtures are downstream dependencies of the test harness that loads YAML under tests/config/hybrid; any changes to fixture structure, file naming, or YAML key semantics can change test selection and expected outcomes.

They likely correspond to upstream parsing/normalization logic in the codebase responsible for translating hybrid configuration definitions into internal record/zone/file targeting used by planning and apply stages.
recommendations: When modifying hybrid configuration parsing/record targeting logic, validate that root-label handling (empty-string owner key) remains consistent with the fixture expectations.
Maintain compatibility with the fixture’s modality cues (flat-zone-file vs split-zone-file) so routing into the correct zone-file context remains stable.
Be cautious about changes that affect deterministic mapping from fixture fields to generated planned/apply outputs, as sentinel/value assertions and targeting context can become brittle.

tests/config/override.description: This folder contains test configuration fixtures used to validate how record overrides are applied in dynamic scenarios. The YAML fixture is specifically tailored to override or extend test record values, ensuring that dynamic record/source processing respects override semantics. It matters because it constrains expected behavior in tests and effectively defines contract-like inputs for the override system.
key_behaviors: Applies YAML-based overrides to dynamic record values (e.g., for A records) during test execution
Replaces or extends baseline fixture data using generic key patterns (e.g., record key 'a' and an 'added' field/value)
Exercises override interaction with dynamic record/dynamic source processing paths rather than static configurations

insights.title: Override YAML defines how dynamic record values are replaced/extended
insights.problem: If the override application logic changes (e.g., merge vs replace semantics, key mapping, or dynamic resolution timing), the fixture expectations will break.
insights.root_cause: The fixture relies on specific override structure and key names ('a', 'added') to trigger deterministic override behavior in the dynamic processing pipeline.
insights.solution: Treat the fixture structure and keys as part of a behavioral contract for the override subsystem; validate that any changes to override parsing/merging preserve the intended semantics for dynamic records.

insights.title: Fixture is coupled to dynamic record/dynamic source processing paths
insights.problem: Modifying dynamic processing (ordering, normalization, or when overrides are applied) can change how this fixture is interpreted.
insights.root_cause: The fixture is explicitly intended to test overrides during dynamic record/dynamic source processing rather than only static record handling.
insights.solution: When editing dynamic processing or override application code, ensure this fixture is still handled consistently with its purpose: overrides must apply correctly in the dynamic path.

insights.title: YAML structure must remain compatible with the test harness loader
insights.problem: Changing fixture schema or formatting can make tests fail due to parsing/loader expectations.
insights.root_cause: The test harness likely expects specific YAML keys and structure to map into record overrides.
insights.solution: Maintain compatibility with the test configuration loader’s expected schema for override fixtures, including key names and nesting implied by how overrides are applied.

key_files.why: Primary fixture that defines override input structure and values for dynamic record override behavior tests.

construct_relationships: The YAML keys/structure in tests/config/override/dynamic.tests.yaml map to override logic that affects dynamic record processing (records such as A are implied by the fixture purpose).
The fixture's use of generic keys ('a' and 'added') implies a deterministic mapping into the override engine’s record attribute/augmentation model during test runs.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: override parsing/loader and dynamic record/dynamic source processing components in the main application must understand and apply this YAML fixture correctly.

Downstream dependency: test assertions depend on the exact result of applying this fixture’s overrides during dynamic processing; any behavioral changes in override application will manifest as test failures.
recommendations: When editing any code involved in parsing, loading, or applying overrides, confirm that dynamic override semantics (replace vs merge/extend) remain consistent with the fixture’s intended structure.

If changing dynamic record/dynamic source processing order or timing (e.g., when overrides are applied relative to dynamic generation), ensure the test fixture still produces the same effective record values.
Do not alter the fixture’s key names or structure without ensuring the test harness and override mapping logic are updated in lockstep; otherwise parsing and mapping will break.
Run the full configuration/override test suite that consumes this fixture to detect regressions in dynamic override behavior.

tests/config/split.description: This directory contains configuration fixture data for the project’s “split” behavior in tests, primarily focusing on how DNS records are loaded, filtered (included/excluded/ignored), and split/merged into resulting zone configuration. It matters because these YAML fixtures form part of the contract for deterministic parsing and behavior across many DNS record types, including structured records and dynamically routed/weighted records. The fixtures also encode scope semantics (octodns.included/excluded/ignored) that the split loader and downstream planning rely on.
key_behaviors: Loads per-record YAML fixtures and converts them into typed DNS record representations suitable for split-based configuration assembly.
Applies octodns-specific record participation controls (ignored/excluded/included-by-scope) during split configuration loading.
Supports deterministic handling of multiple DNS record types with correct TTL, value formats, and structured fields, including proper escaping/serialization for fields like NAPTR regex/flags.
Validates dynamic/weighted geo routing logic for different record types (A, AAAA, CNAME) using pool selection rules with weights and fallbacks.
Validates subzone/split merging behavior by combining minimal per-host YAML fragments across multiple files.

insights.title: YAML structure is the behavioral contract for record parsing
insights.problem: If fixture key names, types, or nesting change, the split loader/parsers may interpret records differently or omit fields, causing planning/output mismatches.
insights.root_cause: Tests depend on deterministic conversion from YAML into record objects and typed fields for many DNS record variants.
insights.solution: When editing or adding fixtures, preserve the exact YAML schema shape and field types expected by the split configuration loader and record parsers.

insights.title: octodns.included / octodns.excluded / octodns.ignored must be interpreted consistently
insights.problem: Scope directives embedded in fixtures can change whether records participate in the resulting zone, leading to missing or extra records in split outputs.
insights.root_cause: The split loader likely applies these directives before record merging/planning, and multiple fixtures cover different directive combinations.
insights.solution: Maintain the directive formats and target hostnames referenced by included/excluded/ignored lists to match the test harness expectations.

insights.title: Dynamic pool selection and weighted candidate values must be preserved across record types
insights.problem: Dynamic A/AAAA/CNAME fixtures likely exercise shared logic for geo-rule evaluation, pool selection, default/fallback behavior, and weight propagation.
insights.root_cause: Record-type-specific value fields (IPv4, IPv6, target hostnames) still share the same dynamic pool/rules/ranking structure.
insights.solution: Keep the geo rules, pool names, and weight distributions structurally consistent with what the dynamic splitting logic expects, including fallback/default pool behavior.

insights.title: NAPTR-style structured fields are sensitive to escaping/serialization
insights.problem: Incorrect escaping within fixture strings can change how the test compares serialized output or normalized values.
insights.root_cause: Record types like NAPTR contain multiple structured attributes (flags/order/preference/regexp/replacement/service) where string content may be normalized or compared verbatim.
insights.solution: Preserve literal contents and escaping sequences in these YAML fixtures as part of test expectations.

insights.title: Subzone split fixtures rely on consistent host identification and merging
insights.problem: If hostnames, record names, or minimal record documents are altered, merging across files can map values to the wrong owner name or omit expected records.
insights.root_cause: Subzone split tests likely combine multiple per-file fragments keyed by record name.
insights.solution: Ensure each per-file fixture continues to define the correct record name and value shape used for subzone-level merge assertions.

key_files.why: Defines a shared TXT record that is conditionally gated by split/shared processing, affecting which records appear under specific test modes.
Exercises dynamic weighted geo behavior for A records, including geo rules, multiple pools, weights, and default fallback.
Validates that the dynamic/weighted geo mechanism works equivalently for AAAA values and preserves weights.
Covers SRV structured fields (priority/weight/port/target), which are commonly sensitive to field parsing and serialization.
Encodes excluded semantics for a specific record name, directly testing record participation filtering during split loading.

construct_relationships: Dynamic fixtures (dynamic.tests.tst/*.yaml) share a common conceptual rule structure (geo rules selecting pools plus weighted candidate values), differing only by record-type-specific value fields (IPv4 vs IPv6 vs CNAME target).

Unit fixtures (unit.tests.tst/*.yaml) establish typed parsing/serialization expectations for multiple DNS RR types, including structured multi-field records; the split mechanism consumes these parsed representations to build the resulting zone configuration.

Filter/participation fixtures (unit.tests.tst/ignored.yaml, excluded.yaml, included.yaml) interact with the split loader’s scope logic to determine whether otherwise-valid record definitions enter the final assembled zone for a test scope.
notable_relationships: These fixtures are downstream dependencies of the test suite that likely exercises octodns split configuration loading and planning; changes here will affect assertions comparing resulting zone objects or rendered outputs.

Dynamic geo-rule fixtures implicitly depend on shared dynamic/geo evaluation code elsewhere in the project; they must remain aligned with the rule schema and default/fallback semantics implemented by the splitter.
recommendations: Preserve the exact YAML schema (key names, nesting, data types like lists vs scalars) expected by the split loader and DNS record parsers, since these fixtures act as behavioral contracts.

When modifying fixture values for dynamic/geo tests, ensure pool/rule relationships and weights remain consistent across record types to avoid unintentional coverage gaps or mismatched selection behavior.
Maintain the scope directive formats and referenced record names/hosts in included/excluded/ignored fixtures, because participation logic determines which records appear in computed outputs.
For structured record fixtures (e.g., NAPTR, SRV), preserve string escaping and structured field composition to avoid altering normalized/serialized comparisons.

octodns/record.description: The `octodns/record` folder defines octoDNS’s core in-memory model for DNS records, including a shared `Record` factory/registry, per-RR-type value models, and common validation/parsing/rendering helpers. It matters because all provider/processor/planner layers rely on these records to normalize inputs (including IDNA), enforce DNS-specific constraints, and serialize canonical rdata for change detection. The folder also centralizes templating behavior at the value/field level so placeholders can be preserved and expanded consistently across record types.
key_behaviors: All record types participate in a central registry keyed by DNS RR type, enabling `Record.new()` and parsing from RRs to select the correct record/value implementation.

Record construction normalizes record names via IDNA, validates structural metadata (e.g., fqdn/name, TTL, healthcheck unless lenient), and then delegates value validation to the specific record/value classes.
Value models implement lifecycle hooks such as `parse_rdata_text`, `validate`, and `template`, producing canonical internal representations and deterministic rdata output.
Chunked/TXT-like and parameterized record types apply specialized canonicalization (e.g., TXT chunking rules, deterministic SVCB/SVCB-param ordering) to ensure stable comparisons and plan generation.

Shared target FQDN validation and “templating bypass” logic is applied consistently for record types with target-like fields (MX/SRV/SVCB/HTTPS and others), including allowance of null targets where applicable.

insights.title: Package-level re-exports establish the public import contract
insights.problem: Callers import record/value classes and exceptions from `octodns.record` rather than per-module paths, so symbol availability and names must remain stable.
insights.root_cause: The package `__init__.py` re-exports many classes/exceptions and intentionally references symbols for linter suppression.
insights.solution: When editing modules, preserve exported names and ensure that any renamed/moved types continue to be re-exported consistently to avoid downstream import breakage.

insights.title: Record.copy/value handling has edge cases around falsey/empty inputs
insights.problem: Incorrect truthiness checks can prevent intended overrides when value(s) are empty strings/lists, producing incorrect record data and potentially incorrect diffs/plans.
insights.root_cause: Value copying previously used truthiness-based branching (`if value:` / `elif values:`), which treats empty-but-valid values as “not provided.”
insights.solution: Maintain correct semantics in any edits that touch record copying or merge behavior: falsey values may still be legitimate and must be propagated into the copied record.

insights.title: Templating bypass/placeholder handling impacts validation and canonical output
insights.problem: Validation and templating interactions can cause validation warnings/errors even when placeholders are present, or can fail to expand placeholders in fields that support templating.
insights.root_cause: Target FQDN validation includes templating bypass rules for `{}` placeholders; multiple record types rely on consistent target/field templating semantics.
insights.solution: When modifying target validation or value templating hooks, keep placeholder detection and bypass conditions aligned with how downstream processors expect placeholders to behave.

insights.title: Deterministic rdata_text generation is required for stable change detection
insights.problem: Non-canonical ordering/escaping or inconsistent serialization can cause checksum mismatches and unnecessary plan updates.
insights.root_cause: Several value types include explicit canonicalization (e.g., TXT chunking/escaping, SVCB/SVCB-param ordering, MX rdata formatting, NAPTR quoting/flag normalization).
insights.solution: Preserve deterministic serialization behavior (including ordering, quoting rules, escaping, and normalization) when editing parsing/validation/rendering.

insights.title: Special-case validators for complex RDATA types must cover malformed escaping and case variation
insights.problem: Some record types may accept malformed inputs or reject otherwise-valid provider variations (e.g., capitalization) if validation isn’t sufficiently comprehensive.
insights.root_cause: Chunked TXT-like validation includes targeted checks for escaping/semicolons/ASCII; NAPTR flag handling includes normalization but may have been incomplete regarding case-insensitivity.
insights.solution: Any edits to these validators should retain coverage for provider variability and malformed escape patterns, because these directly affect plan correctness and error surfacing.

key_files.why: Defines the central `Record` base class, registry/dispatch, record construction/validation, normalization, and generic value combination from parsed RRs.
Centralizes FQDN/target validation and templating bypass behavior used by multiple record types (MX/SRV/SVCB/HTTPS).
Provides shared chunking and validation for TXT/SPF-like chunked string value types, including escape handling and canonical chunk rendering.
Implements RFC9460-style SVCB parsing/validation/serialization for svcpriority/targetname and svcparams, including deterministic parameter ordering and ECH-related checks.
Models create/update/delete deltas derived from record state comparisons; correctness depends on stable record/value equality and canonical data.

construct_relationships: Record registry in `base.py` maps RR types to concrete `*Record` classes via `Record.register_type(...)` (e.g., A/AAAA/CNAME/ALIAS and SVCB/HTTPS variants).
Per-record `*Value` classes integrate with the shared base behaviors by implementing or leveraging shared mixins (e.g., `ValuesMixin`) for parsing, validation, templating, and equality.
Record construction in `base.py` encodes record names via IDNA and delegates value instantiation/validation to the RR-type-specific value classes and their `_value_type` hooks.
Chunked TXT-like values use `_ChunkedValuesMixin` from `chunked.py` (which extends `ValuesMixin`) to produce `chunked_values`/`rr_values` and enforce chunking-specific validation rules.
MX and SRV record types rely on `target.py`-provided target/FQDN validation helpers for their `exchange`/`target` fields, and apply templating to only the appropriate target-like field(s).
HTTPS record type is an alias/specialization of SVCB semantics via `HttpsValue` and `HttpsRecord`, tying into the same shared SVCB value parsing/validation/serialization and the `Record` registry.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependencies (inferred): provider adapters and RR parsing components in other folders must supply raw inputs that these value models normalize, validate, and render deterministically for planning.

Downstream dependencies (inferred): `octodns/processor` templating/filtering and `octodns/manager` planning/apply stages rely on record/value equality, canonical rdata, and consistent templating/placeholder handling to compute correct diffs and avoid spurious updates.
recommendations: Preserve the public import/export contract established by `octodns/record/__init__.py` (class and exception names must remain available at package root).
When editing record construction/copying/merge paths in `base.py`, preserve correct handling of falsey-but-valid values (empty strings/lists) and the intended `value` vs `values` semantics.
Keep templating behavior consistent across shared helpers and record-specific `Value.template` implementations, especially around placeholder `{}` detection and any validation bypass rules.
Maintain deterministic canonical serialization (`rdata_text`, chunking/escaping, quoting, and parameter ordering) because plan generation/change checks depend on stable comparisons.
Any modifications to target FQDN validation logic must remain compatible with all record types that rely on it (MX/SRV/SVCB/HTTPS) including null-target handling and IDNA normalization expectations.

octodns/processor.description: The octodns/processor folder defines the pluggable “zone/plan processors” that run inside octoDNS’ sync lifecycle to transform desired zones, validate constraints, and post-process plan changes. These processors are wired into the pipeline via well-known hooks such as process_source_zone, process_target_zone, process_source_and_target_zones, and process_plan, making them central to how octoDNS enforces manageability rules and correctness before providers see a plan. Because multiple processors mutate shared Zone/Record/Plan structures and can raise processor-specific exceptions, changes in this subtree must respect pipeline ordering, record mutability expectations, and lenient-mode semantics.
key_behaviors: Provide hook-based transformation/validation across distinct pipeline phases (source-only, target reconciliation, combined source/target, and plan filtering).
Mutate desired Zone records in-place (or via replace semantics) to enforce conventions (e.g., TTL ranges, ownership/meta tagging, canonical trailing dots, templated values).
Enforce manageability by adding sentinel/ownership TXT records and filtering plan changes so only explicitly owned records are acted upon.
Perform record content validation using specialized DNS-domain logic (e.g., SPF mechanism/lookup estimation, ACME challenge ownership tagging/cleanup).
Augment zones by generating additional records based on other records (e.g., auto-generating ARPA PTRs from A/AAAA inputs).

insights.title: Lenient-mode affects whether processors validate or skip work
insights.problem: Multiple processors explicitly skip validation when record.lenient is enabled, meaning behavior diverges depending on how/when lenient flags are set and propagated through earlier processors.
insights.root_cause: Processors implement conditional logic tied to record.lenient and sometimes how record copies are created during processing.
insights.solution: When editing any processor here, ensure lenient gating and lenient propagation remain consistent with expected pipeline semantics; confirm that record objects retain lenient flags through transformations.

insights.title: Record values must remain in expected RR value subclasses after normalization
insights.problem: Processors that normalize record fields (notably trailing dot normalization) previously broke downstream expectations by converting RR value objects back to plain strings.
insights.root_cause: Value normalization can accidentally change type identity (e.g., subclasses for CNAME/ALIAS/PTR) even if string content is correct.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve RR value subclasses and reconstruct values using the intended class when transforming fields; confirm downstream serialization/planning still receives correct types.

insights.title: Ownership tagging uses sentinel TXT records and must be consistent across planning filters
insights.problem: Ownership and ACME managing processors rely on specific TXT naming/value conventions (sentinel markers and encoded ownership names). Incorrect matching can cause octoDNS to delete unowned ACME TXT or filter out legitimate plan changes.
insights.root_cause: Plan filtering infers ownership by scanning existing+desired records and comparing against generated ownership/sentinel signatures.
insights.solution: Any changes must preserve the exact ownership TXT formats, sentinel markers, and how wildcard ownership names are encoded; ensure reconciliation logic remains aligned with tagging behavior.

insights.title: Alias zone processing can cause duplicate ownership TXT emission
insights.problem: OwnershipProcessor can emit ownership TXT records twice when processing alias zones (original + alias), leading to duplicate record exceptions and failed plans.
insights.root_cause: Processor logic may run for both alias and original targets, generating identical ownership records if not guarded.
insights.solution: Edits should maintain the deduplication/should_replace behavior around alias-zone double-processing and ensure record replacement semantics remain safe.

insights.title: Configuration validation and up-to-date detection are sensitive to boundary conditions
insights.problem: TTL clamp/restriction processors and MetaProcessor have correctness risks when configuration is invalid (e.g., min/max TTL ordering) or when up-to-date detection logic doesn’t align with actual plan change shapes.
insights.root_cause: Validation and “up-to-date” checks use assumptions about equality semantics and planned record change granularity.
insights.solution: Maintain strict configuration checks and ensure meta up-to-date logic compares the right dimensions (e.g., key-based vs value-based) so single-change scenarios don’t produce incorrect suppression or false positives.

insights.title: Record filtering must handle missing/None record values safely
insights.problem: Filter processors can crash if record.value is None while value-based allow/reject logic assumes presence of value.rdata_text.
insights.root_cause: Value filters access value representations without guarding against None/empty value variants.
insights.solution: Ensure all filter matching logic tolerates None/empty values in the same way across type/name/value filters, especially for multi-value and structured value record types.

insights.category: DNS logic recursion and external lookups
insights.title: SPF processor performs recursive include resolution via dnspython and must bound cost/behavior
insights.problem: SPF validation inspects TXT records, counts DNS lookup cost, rejects deprecated mechanisms (ptr), and recursively resolves include: mechanisms using dnspython lookups; errors or recursion depth issues can break processing.
insights.root_cause: SPF semantics require multiple mechanisms/modifiers parsing and potentially recursive DNS queries.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve recursion/lookup counting behavior and exception mapping so pipeline failures and lenient handling remain predictable under real DNS conditions.

insights.title: ACME managing processor must coordinate desired tagging and target cleanup across hooks
insights.problem: The ACME processor tags desired TXT values with a sentinel and then deletes existing ACME TXT records that don’t match ownership; any mismatch can either prevent claiming ownership or cause unintended deletions.
insights.root_cause: Ownership is represented by modifying desired record values and tracking tagged values in an internal set, then applying deletion decisions during target reconciliation.
insights.solution: Preserve the sentinel encoding, internal ownership tracking behavior, and deletion criteria across process_source_zone vs process_target_zone.

insights.title: Auto-ARPA PTR generation is deterministic but constrained by priority/limits
insights.problem: AutoArpa converts IP-to-FQDN mappings into PTR candidates, orders/uniquifies targets, and selects based on priority and max limits; non-deterministic selection could cause plan churn.
insights.root_cause: Selection logic depends on candidate ordering, deduplication, and configured priority/limit parameters.
insights.solution: Edits must keep ordering/uniquing deterministic and maintain respect for wildcard handling, priority, and max_auto_arpa constraints.

key_files.why: Defines the processor abstraction and the hook/exception contract all other processors rely on.
Implements the ownership TXT tagging and plan-change filtering that governs whether records are considered manageable.
Implements ACME DNS-01 challenge TXT claiming via sentinel tagging and deletion suppression/cleanup during target reconciliation.
Normalizes DNS target/value formatting while preserving RR value subclass types—high impact on downstream plan generation.
Performs SPF-specific parsing and DNS lookup counting with recursive include resolution, raising dedicated exceptions on validation failures.

construct_relationships: BaseProcessor hooks (process_source_zone/process_target_zone/process_source_and_target_zones/process_plan) are implemented by specialized processors (e.g., OwnershipProcessor, AcmeManagingProcessor, EnsureTrailingDots, MetaProcessor, Templating, AutoArpa) and must be invoked by the surrounding pipeline in a way that respects each processor’s phase expectations.
OwnershipProcessor’s process_source_zone creates ownership TXT records that are later used by process_plan to filter plan changes to only owned records (ownership signature/name matching).

AcmeManagingProcessor’s process_source_zone appends a sentinel marker to desired ACME TXT values and tracks them in an internal owned set; process_target_zone then uses the owned set and sentinel criteria to decide which existing ACME TXT records to delete or ignore.

EnsureTrailingDots transforms desired record.value/record.values fields (ALIAS/CNAME/DNAME/NS/PTR/MX/SRV) and uses desired.add_record(... replace=True ...) such that subsequent processors and provider planning operate on normalized values with correct RR value types.

Templating constructs a parameter dictionary from zone/record metadata and templates record.value or each item in record.values, influencing downstream processors that validate or generate data based on templated outputs (e.g., SPF, ownership tagging, TTL clamping/restriction).
notable_relationships: These processors operate downstream of configuration and upstream of provider planning/apply logic; their mutations feed directly into Zone/Plan objects consumed by octodns/provider/* and octodns/manager planning.

ProcessorException and specialized exceptions (RestrictionException, TtlArgumentException, SpfValueException/SpfDnsLookupException, ProcessorException, TemplatingError) propagate into the pipeline’s error-handling and influence whether processing aborts vs continues in lenient scenarios.

DNS-specific processors (SpfDnsLookupProcessor, AutoArpa) depend on external/environmental behavior: SPF relies on dnspython TXT lookups during processing; ARPA generation depends on correct IP parsing and on how zones are enumerated to find matching reverse zones.
recommendations: When modifying any processor hook, preserve the expected phase semantics and ensure any internal state used across hooks (e.g., ACME owned set) remains consistent with how the pipeline instantiates/reuses processor objects.
Maintain lenient-mode behavior: validators here must continue to skip or enforce constraints based on record.lenient and ensure transformations don’t accidentally remove lenient flags.
Preserve RR value type identities when normalizing or mutating record fields (especially trailing dot normalization) so downstream serialization and plan diffs remain stable.
Keep manageability signatures consistent across processors: ownership TXT naming/value conventions and ACME sentinel tagging/deletion criteria must remain aligned with plan filtering logic.
Confirm that changes don’t introduce duplicate record creation risks in alias-zone contexts where processors may run more than once for overlapping logical targets.
If editing SPF logic, respect existing recursion/lookup counting and exception behavior since it affects pipeline failures and operational safety under real DNS conditions.

octodns/provider.description: The octodns/provider folder defines the core abstractions and implementations that turn source and desired DNS zone data into a provider-specific change plan, optionally rendering that plan for humans and applying it to targets. This layer matters because it coordinates capability filtering, processor hook execution, record-level diffing, plan safety enforcement, and provider integration points like YAML-backed targets. Changes here have cross-cutting effects on planning correctness, backward compatibility with processors/providers, and the stability/format of plan outputs consumed elsewhere.
key_behaviors: Provider planning orchestrates a multi-stage normalization pipeline for both desired and existing zones, including provider capability filtering and provider-specific meta changes
Processor hooks are executed in two phases tied to target-zone and combined source/target context, with special handling for lenient/backward-compatible processor signatures
Record-level changes are computed deterministically and filtered for provider false-positives before being assembled into a Plan object
Plans enforce safety constraints to prevent excessive update/delete operations based on existing zone size thresholds and special-cased handling of root NS changes
YAML provider maps YAML zone/record representations into internal Zone/Record objects for populate (read) and applies post-plan state back into YAML (write), with deterministic serialization

insights.title: Lenient mode and processor hook signature compatibility are required for runtime stability
insights.problem: Provider processing and processor hooks rely on a `lenient` mode being passed through, but older processors may not accept the `lenient` keyword argument.
insights.root_cause: API evolution introduced/expanded a `lenient` parameter without guaranteeing all installed/legacy processors support it.
insights.solution: Any changes must preserve the current backward-compatible dispatch behavior (fallback when `lenient` is not accepted) while ensuring lenient semantics are consistently applied across both desired and existing-zone preprocessing.

insights.title: Root NS support handling can conflict with strict vs warn/except behaviors
insights.problem: Root NS-related warnings/behavior may not be fully suppressible and may not consistently follow `strict_supports` semantics when providers cannot manage root NS.
insights.root_cause: Root NS is treated specially across provider processing and safety/plan logic, but the warning/suppression pathway does not consistently respect strict capability policies.
insights.solution: Edits must respect the interplay between provider capability filtering, root NS warning suppression, and plan safety special-casing to avoid changing enforcement outcomes.

insights.title: Plan output serialization/renderers include provider meta and may break downstream expectations
insights.problem: Plan renderers now include provider meta sections, and HTML/Markdown/JSON consumers may not expect additional keys/sections.
insights.root_cause: Plan output schema evolved to include meta information for provider context.
insights.solution: Any modifications to plan data structures or renderers must preserve the serialized structure expected by existing consumers, including inclusion/exclusion of `meta`.

insights.title: Safety constraints depend on existing zone record count and special-casing
insights.problem: Plan safety checks use update/delete percentage thresholds gated on minimum existing-record count; root NS changes are handled as always unsafe.
insights.root_cause: Safety enforcement logic mixes threshold gating, percent-based limits, and special-case behavior for root NS record changes.
insights.solution: Edits must keep the gating conditions and root NS special-casing aligned with expected operational safety semantics.

insights.title: YAML missing-zone behavior can unexpectedly fail runs
insights.problem: YamlProvider used in pipelines where some zone files may be absent can fail the whole run when YAML is missing, even when that absence should be allowable for some sources.
insights.root_cause: Current YAML discovery/population flow likely assumes the presence of all expected zones for the configured provider directories.
insights.solution: Changes must preserve or correctly implement the intended missing-zone handling behavior (e.g., any configured ignore behavior) so pipeline-level assumptions remain stable.

insights.title: Semicolon escaping logic can be incorrect under `escaped_semicolons=false` and double-escaped inputs
insights.problem: When escaped semicolons are disabled, TXT/SPF inputs containing semicolons can be mishandled due to incorrect key transformation, and double-escaped semicolons (`\;`) may be interpreted incorrectly by downstream consumers.
insights.root_cause: Normalization/escaping logic likely manipulates YAML value structures and/or performs parsing that differs based on configuration, with an incorrect reference to a key when escaping is disabled.
insights.solution: Any edits around YAML parsing/normalization must keep TXT/SPF escaping semantics consistent for both enabled/disabled modes and ensure double-escaped representations are treated correctly.

insights.title: Deterministic record serialization and default TTL omission are required
insights.problem: YAML provider serializes post-apply zone state deterministically and may omit default TTL values.
insights.root_cause: Determinism and canonicalization are often required for stable diffs and repeatable operations.
insights.solution: Edits must maintain ordering determinism and TTL omission rules so produced YAML remains predictable.

key_files.why: Defines BaseProvider, the core planning workflow coordinator that normalizes zones, invokes processor hooks, computes and filters changes, and constructs Plan objects.
Implements Plan safety checks and all plan output renderers/serializations, which affects both operational safety and the external plan reporting format.
Provides YamlProvider that integrates file-backed YAML sources/targets into the provider planning/apply pipeline, including parsing and writing record state.
Defines the provider exception types used to signal unsupported features and provider-level errors across integrations.

construct_relationships: BaseProvider (base.py) depends on Plan (plan.py) to encapsulate computed changes and enforce safety via Plan.raise_if_unsafe during/after planning

BaseProvider extends/reuses behaviors from octodns.source.base.BaseSource (inferred from description) for populating existing/target zone state, then layers provider-specific processing for desired and existing zones

YamlProvider (yaml.py) implements the provider interface implied by BaseProvider, using populate to read YAML into Zone/Record objects and apply/_apply to write provider-post-change zone state back to YAML
Plan renderers in plan.py consume Plan.data and include provider meta, meaning BaseProvider must supply consistent meta fields expected by renderers
notable_relationships: Provider planning is part of a larger pipeline that includes octodns/context and octodns/manager orchestration, implying changes here can affect CLI and orchestration-level behavior/expectations
BaseProvider’s two-phase processor hook execution implies upstream integration with octodns/processor; any signature/kwarg changes must remain compatible with existing processor implementations
YamlProvider’s parsing/serialization behavior depends on internal Record/Zone models and Record.registered_types(), so changes must align with those canonical representations used across the project
Plan outputs are likely consumed by CLI/reporting layers (and potentially external integrations), so serialized formats and filenames/paths used by output classes must remain compatible
recommendations: When editing BaseProvider logic, preserve backward compatibility for processor hook invocation (including conditional `lenient` kwarg handling) and ensure lenient semantics propagate consistently through both desired and existing-zone preprocessing paths
Maintain the established interaction between root NS capability handling (warnings/strict_supports behavior) and Plan safety special-casing to avoid changing operational enforcement outcomes
Preserve the Plan data schema and render output expectations (including inclusion of provider meta) and ensure any changes to output filename/path naming remain compatible with calling code/configs

When editing YamlProvider parsing/normalization/apply behavior, keep TXT/SPF semicolon escaping semantics correct for both `escaped_semicolons` modes and handle double-escaped inputs without corrupting values

Ensure YAML file discovery/population/apply continues to meet pipeline assumptions about missing zones (including any configuration controlling missing-zone tolerance) so runs do not fail unexpectedly

tests/config.description: tests/config contains YAML fixture inputs that drive the test suite’s end-to-end verification of configuration parsing, validation, and key behavioral toggles. These fixtures focus on correctness and failure modes across zone aliasing, dynamic ARPA generation, hybrid TXT handling, dry-run precedence, processor/plugin registration, and configuration/plugin import validation. Because these are fixtures, they implicitly define a behavioral contract that production config-loading and planning code must satisfy.
key_behaviors: Resolves zone alias chains and detects alias cycles to prevent infinite recursion during planning
Applies zone-level configuration overrides (notably always-dry-run) with correct precedence across nested zone scopes
Validates configuration schema for pluggable components (providers and plan_outputs), surfacing clear errors for missing/invalid fields and bad import paths
Attaches and validates record processors for dump operations, including graceful handling of unknown/invalid processor references
Generates dynamic reverse DNS (ARPA) zones/records based on auto-arpa rules, including cases where the normal input source is absent
Loads and tolerates optional/empty configuration sections (e.g., empty mappings for processors/plan_outputs/secret_handlers) without crashing
Supports hybrid configuration semantics for TXT records, including correct handling of the root label (empty owner name) and routing between flat vs split zone-file sources

insights.title: plan_outputs and providers must fail loudly on invalid configuration
insights.problem: Fixtures intentionally provide malformed plan_outputs and provider class specifications.
insights.root_cause: Validation and dynamic import resolution must enforce required fields (e.g., plan output class) and fully qualified import paths (module + class).
insights.solution: When editing config-loading/validation code, preserve error-triggering behavior and ensure exceptions/diagnostics remain consistent with what these fixtures expect.

insights.title: Zone alias resolution must detect loops deterministically
insights.problem: There is an alias chain fixture that aliases back through another zone, forming a cycle.
insights.root_cause: Alias resolution can recurse indefinitely if cycle detection is not implemented or if visited-state is not tracked across alias expansion steps.
insights.solution: Maintain cycle-detection semantics so the planning phase terminates and produces stable failure/suppression behavior under cyclic aliases.

insights.title: dry-run toggles vary by zone scope
insights.problem: Fixtures set always-dry-run true for one zone and false for a subzone.
insights.root_cause: Precedence logic must correctly choose the most specific applicable setting while still respecting parent/ancestor configuration.
insights.solution: Preserve override precedence rules for zone-scoped settings when changing any configuration-merging logic.

insights.title: Empty/None configuration sections must be handled safely
insights.problem: A fixture exercises dynamic config fields where some keys may be None or empty mappings.
insights.root_cause: Config loaders may assume presence of keys/structures and break when values are empty or omitted.
insights.solution: Keep compatibility with optional keys so that empty mappings and missing sections do not cause type errors during parsing/initialization.

insights.title: auto-arpa generation works with and without normal input sources
