insights.problem: Fixtures cover auto-arpa behavior when a standard “normal” source is missing versus present, and include placeholder empty outputs for specific reverse domains.
insights.root_cause: Record generation logic must match reverse-domain patterns, emit deterministic records only when inputs satisfy matching rules, and avoid producing output for domains expected to be empty.
insights.solution: Ensure dynamic ARPA generation and reverse-domain matching semantics remain stable, especially for the “no output” scenarios represented by empty fixtures.

insights.title: Dump processors must support allowlists and detect unknown processors
insights.problem: A fixture uses a processor allowlist for one zone and references an unknown processor in another.
insights.root_cause: Processor registration and lookup must integrate with zone selection/scope and enforce validity of referenced processors.
insights.solution: When adjusting processor registration or dump pipeline code, ensure unknown processor references continue to trigger the expected error-handling behavior.

insights.title: TXT hybrid naming supports empty owner label (root)
insights.problem: Hybrid fixtures include TXT records where the owner label is an empty string (root label).
insights.root_cause: Name/label normalization must treat empty owner labels consistently and map them to the correct zone origin.
insights.solution: Preserve normalization and zone-file targeting behavior for root-label records across flat-zone-file and split-zone-file hybrid modes.

key_files.why: Defines cyclic alias chains that constrain alias-resolution termination and correctness.
Establishes dry-run precedence/override behavior at parent vs subzone scope.
Represents provider import/path validation failure for non-existent module/class targets.
Validates dynamic reverse DNS generation when normal sources and auto-arpa rules interact.
Covers hybrid TXT record mapping for the root label (empty owner name) and expected targeting.

construct_relationships: Alias resolution logic in the core planning flow must interpret zone alias definitions coming from tests/config/alias-zone-loop.yaml and prevent recursion by tracking visited zones during alias expansion.

Configuration merging logic must combine global and zone-scoped settings so that always-dry-run in tests/config/always-dry-run.yaml yields different effective dry-run behavior for a zone and its subzone.

Config loader/plugin registry components must parse plan_outputs/provider class specifications from tests/config/bad-plan-output-*.yaml and tests/config/bad-provider-*.yaml, enforcing required fields and valid import paths.
Dump pipeline must construct processor chains based on tests/config/dump-processors.yaml, including correct allowlist behavior and predictable handling when a referenced processor is unknown.
Dynamic ARPA generation code must use fixtures in tests/config/dynamic-arpa/* to decide when to emit PTR records and when to emit no output, matching reverse-domain pattern expectations.

notable_relationships.relationship: tests/config fixtures depend on shared YAML parsing and custom tag/include mechanics
notable_relationships.inferred_from: The test suite includes a dedicated include-fixture directory and other fixtures rely on consistent YAML parsing behavior.

notable_relationships.relationship: Dynamic ARPA fixtures likely feed into Zone/Provider planning code paths rather than isolated parsers
notable_relationships.inferred_from: The fixtures encode deterministic expected record outcomes including explicit empty outputs for certain reverse domains.

notable_relationships.relationship: Hybrid fixtures constrain how record ownership (labels) is normalized and mapped into zone-file contexts
notable_relationships.inferred_from: Hybrid fixtures cover root label and multiple hybrid source modalities (flat-zone-file vs split-zone-file).

recommendations: When modifying configuration parsing/merging, preserve zone-scoped precedence semantics as encoded by always-dry-run.yaml, and ensure parent/child interactions remain consistent.
When modifying alias expansion or zone planning, maintain cycle-detection behavior required by alias-zone-loop.yaml so tests terminate and outcomes remain deterministic.
When changing plugin/validator code paths (providers and plan_outputs), ensure validation triggers and error conditions remain compatible with the malformed configurations in bad-*.yaml fixtures.
When editing dynamic ARPA and reverse-domain matching logic, preserve “no output” behavior for the placeholder empty fixtures and maintain determinism for generated PTR-related outputs.
When altering processor and dump wiring, keep processor lookup/registration and unknown-processor error handling consistent with dump-processors.yaml.

octodns/cmds.description: The octodns/cmds folder defines the CLI surface area for octoDNS operations such as planning/dumping/comparing/synchronizing DNS state and producing resolver-consistency reports. It acts as the glue layer between user inputs (CLI args), configuration loading (via the Manager), and execution/reporting logic that lives in other octoDNS modules. Because these commands orchestrate concurrency, logging, output formats, and exit codes, changes in this subtree must preserve observable CLI behavior and integration contracts with the rest of the system.
key_behaviors: Parse common flags for version and logging setup, then ensure logging level/handlers match user-selected verbosity and destinations
Load octoDNS Manager configuration and resolve named providers/sources for a requested zone
Populate a Zone using configured sources (optionally in lenient mode) before any compare/dump/report operations
Compute diffs or validation outcomes and convert them into user-visible output plus process exit codes suitable for automation
Perform asynchronous network-driven reporting (DNS resolver queries) with bounded concurrency and structured output (CSV/JSON)

insights.title: Resolver report can emit invalid output when resolver answers are missing
insights.problem: When a resolver produced no entries for a record/type, aggregation logic could attempt to iterate/join None or otherwise mishandle empty results, leading to errors or malformed CSV/JSON output.
insights.root_cause: Missing-answer handling and resolver membership checks were too permissive/incorrect, causing inconsistent data structures (e.g., None vs empty list) and brittle exception-to-output mapping.
insights.solution: Ensure all resolver results normalize to stable iterable structures (e.g., empty list) and make resolver membership and exception mapping explicit so output fields remain well-typed and consistent.

insights.title: Report command relies on asyncio concurrency limiting and async DNS queries
insights.problem: Concurrency and exception handling determine both performance and reliability of resolver reporting; incorrect task orchestration or broad exception handling risks partial/incorrect aggregation and inconsistent logging.
insights.root_cause: Asynchronous DNS resolution plus per-(record,resolver) task creation requires consistent timeouts, sentinel values, and concurrency control (Semaphore) to prevent overwhelming resolvers or event loop.
insights.solution: Preserve the concurrency model (global limiter), timeout behavior, and explicit mapping of dnspython exceptions into deterministic report artifacts.

insights.title: Sync command gates changes with dry-run/doit and optional checksum behavior
insights.problem: Accidentally applying an unexpected plan is prevented by requiring explicit user intent and, optionally, a checksum match.
insights.root_cause: Sync command behavior is parameterized by CLI flags (dry-run vs apply, checksum gate) and must align with how Manager computes plans/checksums.
insights.solution: Maintain the coupling between CLI flags and Manager.sync safety checks so that exit status and application/dry-run behavior remains deterministic across versions.

insights.title: Validate command treats warnings as fatal via a custom logging handler
insights.problem: Validation failures are determined by whether warnings are emitted to a specific logger; changing log routing/handler behavior can break the intended failure semantics.
insights.root_cause: Flagging behavior depends on attaching a handler to the 'Record' logger and counting warnings as validation issues.
insights.solution: Keep logger targeting, handler behavior, and exit code mapping stable so CI/automation continues to interpret validation outcomes correctly.

insights.title: CLI logging setup supports both YAML dictConfig and built-in handlers
insights.problem: If a YAML logging config is provided, the command must bypass built-in StreamHandler/SysLogHandler setup to avoid conflicting handlers/levels.
insights.root_cause: Logging initialization branches based on the presence of --logging-config; root logger levels are then overridden by debug/quiet flags.
insights.solution: Preserve the precedence rules: apply YAML first (via dictConfig) and only then adjust levels, or otherwise use the built-in handler selection logic exactly as users expect.

key_files.why: Centralizes shared CLI argument parsing and logging initialization, affecting every command's runtime behavior and observable logs.
Implements the most complex orchestration: async DNS querying across record/resolver pairs with concurrency limits and CSV/JSON output.
Defines apply safety semantics (dry-run vs apply, checksum gating) that must remain consistent with Manager.sync.
Implements validation failure behavior by converting log warnings into non-zero exit codes via a custom handler.
Defines how differences between sources are computed, filtered, printed, and turned into process exit status for automation.

construct_relationships: All subcommands use the CLI parsing/logging utilities (directly or indirectly) to ensure consistent logging destinations and verbosity semantics.
Each command (compare/dump/sync/validate/versions/report) loads octoDNS configuration by instantiating a Manager, then performs operations using Manager-provided provider/source registries.
compare/dump/report rely on populating a Zone from one or more sources before computing diffs, emitting dumps, or running resolver consistency checks.
validate relies on Manager.validate_configs and then derives pass/fail solely from emitted warnings captured by the custom logging handler on the Record logger.
report creates per-(record,resolver) async tasks and aggregates results into an in-memory structure before serialization to CSV/JSON; error/exception mapping influences the serialized values.
notable_relationships: Downstream dependency: these CLI modules depend on octodns.manager.Manager for configuration loading, provider resolution by name, and core operations (dump/validate/sync/compare/planning).
Downstream dependency: report depends on octoDNS Zone and source.populate to materialize record sets prior to querying resolvers.
Downstream dependency: report uses dnspython asynchronous querying semantics; its exception types and response shapes must match what dnspython provides for stable sentinel/output handling.
Upstream integration concern: process exit codes and output formats (CSV/JSON/YAML dump structure) are part of the user automation surface and must remain backward compatible.
recommendations: Preserve logging precedence and handler behavior in args.py (YAML dictConfig vs built-in handlers, and how debug/quiet override levels) since downstream commands rely on consistent logs.

When editing report.py, maintain the determinism of task orchestration (asyncio + semaphore concurrency limiting), timeout behavior, and the exact structure of aggregated results so CSV/JSON output remains well-typed even when DNS answers are missing.

Keep compare/dump/sync output and exit-code semantics aligned with how automation consumes them (e.g., compare and validate exit non-zero on detected changes/warnings; sync respects dry-run/doit and checksum gating).
Ensure any changes that affect logger names/handlers (especially validate.py) do not break the warning-to-exit-code contract.
Any modifications that change CLI argument names, defaults, or parsing behavior must be checked for compatibility with how Manager expects parameters (zones/sources/lenient flags/checksum gates).

octodns/secret.description: The octodns/secret folder defines the secrets subsystem used to obtain sensitive configuration values at runtime. It provides a small abstraction layer (base class and exceptions) and an environment-backed implementation that resolves configured secret names into values from process environment variables, including optional inline default support. This matters because other parts of the system (e.g., configuration/manager/CLI) rely on consistent exception types and predictable value parsing semantics.
key_behaviors: Secret providers expose a consistent interface via a shared base class that standardizes logger context and stores the configured secret identifier.
Environment-backed secrets resolve a requested secret name to an environment variable, returning the environment value when present.
Environment-backed secrets support an inline default syntax embedded in the configured name (slash-delimited), using the default only when the env var is missing.
When env var is present, any configured default is ignored and the env value is used exclusively.
Resolved secret values undergo best-effort type coercion: numeric conversion is attempted (float when '.' is present, otherwise int), otherwise the value remains a string.
Failures to resolve a secret from the environment produce a dedicated secrets exception type when no default is available.

insights.title: Pluralization and naming consistency affects callers
insights.problem: Exception naming in this subsystem has been inconsistent (singular vs plural) and those differences can propagate into other components and test expectations.
insights.root_cause: The base exception type naming for secret failures did not match the plural convention used by other secrets-related classes/modules.
insights.solution: When editing or integrating with this subtree, preserve the established exception class name(s) and ensure any caller/test that catches the exception continues to match the correct type.

insights.title: Inline default syntax is part of the public behavior
insights.problem: Configured secret names can include a slash-delimited fallback, and the resolution logic depends on splitting on the first '/'.
insights.root_cause: The environment provider uses parsing logic embedded in the secret name string rather than separate default configuration.
insights.solution: Maintain the exact parsing semantics (split on first '/', default only when env var missing) to avoid breaking configurations that rely on this format.

insights.title: Numeric parsing can change downstream behavior
insights.problem: Resolved secret strings may be converted to float or int depending on the presence of '.', which can affect consumers expecting string values.
insights.root_cause: The environment provider performs best-effort numeric coercion after retrieval.
insights.solution: Treat the numeric coercion rules (float when '.' in string, else int; otherwise keep string) as a compatibility contract when modifying fetch behavior.

insights.title: BaseSecrets establishes logger scoping
insights.problem: All secret provider implementations likely rely on a per-instance logger configured in the base class for consistent logging structure.
insights.root_cause: The base class initializes logger context and stores the secret name for subclasses.
insights.solution: Preserve base class initialization behavior so that provider logging continues to include the intended context fields.

key_files.why: Implements the environment-backed secret resolution logic, including default parsing and numeric coercion semantics.
Defines the shared BaseSecrets abstraction that standardizes logger setup and secret name storage for concrete backends.
Defines the dedicated SecretsException type that callers should catch to distinguish secret resolution failures.
Marks the package; impacts import paths and module resolution but currently has no runtime code.

construct_relationships: EnvironSecrets.fetch(...) raises the secrets exception defined in octodns/secret/exception.py when resolution fails and no default is provided.
EnvironSecrets is expected to be a concrete secret provider that relies on shared initialization behavior from BaseSecrets in octodns/secret/base.py (logger context and stored secret name).
The naming and importability of the exception type in octodns/secret/exception.py are part of the implicit contract with upstream callers that perform exception handling.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: Other system components that request secrets from providers (via manager/config/CLI layers) depend on the exception type and resolution semantics exposed here.
Downstream dependency: Any consumers of secret values depend on the best-effort type coercion behavior (int/float vs string) and must be compatible with those outcomes.
recommendations: When changing any secret-resolution logic, preserve the default syntax behavior: split on the first '/', use the default only when the environment variable is missing, and ignore defaults when the env var is present.
Preserve numeric coercion rules and their thresholds (float if '.' present, otherwise int; fallback to string) to avoid breaking value-type expectations downstream.
Maintain the dedicated exception type(s) used by the secrets subsystem and ensure any changes do not break upstream exception handling or test assertions.
Preserve BaseSecrets initialization semantics for logger scoping and secret-name storage so that logging output and any contextual behavior remain consistent across secret provider implementations.

docs/api.description: The docs/api folder contains Sphinx documentation index and stub files that expose OctoDNS’s internal Python APIs to the published documentation site. Its primary role is to centralize navigation via autosummary directives for major subsystems (CLI commands, managers, processors, providers, records, sources, secrets, and Zone). Changes in the underlying Python modules must remain compatible with how these stubs reference modules and generated autosummary pages.
key_behaviors: Uses Sphinx autosummary to generate API reference stubs for specified modules and symbols
Provides consistent entry points for distinct OctoDNS subsystems (CLI, manager, processors, providers, records, sources, secrets, Zone)
Documents configuration conventions for processors (e.g., top-level processors key and naming references) in addition to API surface

insights.title: Module discovery is driven by autosummary targets
insights.problem: Autosummary entries determine what the docs build attempts to import and document; mismatches can break or silently omit documentation.
insights.root_cause: The stub/index files enumerate module paths and expect Sphinx/autosummary to successfully resolve them at build time.
insights.solution: When editing any referenced module names/paths or exported symbols, ensure Sphinx configuration and importability remain consistent so docs generation continues to work.

insights.title: CLI command modules are treated as public API docs
insights.problem: Renaming, moving, or changing the callable targets used for CLI command documentation can desynchronize the docs from actual behavior.
insights.root_cause: docs/api/cmds.rst is intended as a stable index for CLI entry points like dump/report/sync/validate/versions.
insights.solution: Maintain alignment between CLI entry point locations and what the autosummary indexes reference so the docs continue to match the implemented commands.

insights.title: Processors/providers/sources/secrets are documented as separate API groups
insights.problem: Inconsistent or incomplete module export structures can lead to partially generated or confusing documentation.
insights.root_cause: Each .rst file acts as an aggregation point for a specific subsystem’s module namespace.
insights.solution: Preserve the expected package/module structure for each subsystem (or update the docs index targets consistently with it) to keep API reference completeness.

insights.title: Processor configuration semantics are described explicitly
insights.problem: If processor configuration keys or naming behaviors change, the docs may become inaccurate even if the code still functions.
insights.root_cause: docs/api/processors.rst includes narrative guidance about how to declare processors and reference them by name.
insights.solution: Verify that any runtime/config parsing changes for processors remain consistent with what the documentation claims, especially around how processors are attached at manager-level vs zone-level.

insights.title: Records API reference is comprehensive
insights.problem: Changing record class locations, names, or exports can cause autosummary omissions or build failures.
insights.root_cause: docs/api/records.rst enumerates many record type modules/classes to centralize API navigation.
insights.solution: Ensure record module paths and the documented public classes remain consistent with the autosummary targets.

key_files.why: Defines the API documentation surface for CLI command entry points via autosummary.
Documents processor configuration semantics and provides autosummary navigation for processor modules.
Aggregates API documentation entries for provider modules, including base/plan/yaml variants.
Centralizes API reference navigation for zone data source backends.
Aggregates documentation for the suite of supported DNS record types.

construct_relationships: Sphinx autosummary in each .rst file depends on the corresponding Python module namespace being importable and correctly named so generated stubs map to real objects
docs/api/processors.rst aligns written configuration semantics with the processor subsystem’s expected configuration keys/naming behavior in the runtime code
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: all autosummary targets implicitly rely on the Python package structure under the octodns.* namespace being stable and import-safe during documentation builds

Downstream dependency: the narrative and indexing conventions in these docs are consumed by users as the authoritative API reference, so behavioral/documentation mismatches are user-facing even if the build succeeds
recommendations: When modifying or renaming any referenced octodns.* modules/symbols, verify the docs build still resolves autosummary targets (importability and naming stability).
If processor configuration behavior or key semantics change, ensure the documented expectations in docs/api/processors.rst remain accurate relative to runtime behavior.
Treat these docs/.rst files as part of the public documentation surface: changes to module paths, exports, or package structure should be co-considered with their corresponding API index entries.
Run the Sphinx documentation build after any codebase changes that affect the octodns APIs referenced by these autosummary stubs.

octodns/source.description: The octodns/source folder defines the abstraction and concrete implementations for ingesting external DNS “source of truth” data into octoDNS Zones. It centralizes a Source contract (capabilities and a populate method) and provides implementations that translate external formats (TinyDNS) or runtime inputs (environment variables) into octoDNS Record objects. This matters because the rest of the system (manager/provider/processor) relies on each Source to advertise supported record types and to populate Zones in a consistent, conflict-aware way.
key_behaviors: Expose a uniform contract for sources via a BaseSource interface that advertises supported capabilities and validates whether a record is supported
Translate external representations (TinyDNS line syntax or environment variables) into standardized octoDNS records during Zone population
Support optional feature flags (e.g., Geo, dynamic subnet behavior) through class-level attributes used by the manager to decide compatibility
Perform source-specific error handling for missing runtime inputs (env vars) or parsing/interpretation mismatches (TinyDNS symbol/type mapping)

insights.title: BaseSource defines the mandatory lifecycle and capability metadata
insights.problem: Different source implementations must be discoverable and safely usable by the manager/zone population pipeline.
insights.root_cause: The folder uses a shared abstract base class that requires consistent class attributes (support flags) and a populate implementation.
insights.solution: When editing or adding sources, ensure the BaseSource contract remains consistent: populate must create/insert records into the Zone in the expected way, and supports(record) must correctly reflect capability metadata.

insights.title: EnvVarSource produces deterministic TXT records but depends on external environment state
insights.problem: Environment-dependent configuration can cause hard failures or inconsistent outcomes across runs.
insights.root_cause: EnvVarSource reads a named environment variable at populate time and throws EnvironmentVariableNotFoundException when absent.
insights.solution: Changes must preserve the exception behavior and the exact record shape (TXT record construction from the env value), since upstream logic may rely on exceptions and collision handling being deferred to the manager.

insights.title: TinyDNS parsing hinges on symbol-to-record-type mapping and line-to-record translation
insights.problem: TinyDNS source files must be parsed deterministically and translated into the correct octoDNS record types.
insights.root_cause: TinyDnsBaseSource relies on a SYMBOL_MAP and parsing logic that yields Record.new calls; wrong mapping or parsing changes can silently alter record semantics.
insights.solution: Edits to parsing or mapping logic must maintain compatibility with existing TinyDNS inputs and the established symbol/type behaviors, including uniqueness handling (_unique) and the structure expected by Zone population.

insights.title: TinyDnsFileSource likely uses caching and bulk line loading
insights.problem: Large TinyDNS directories or repeated population runs can impact performance and determinism.
insights.root_cause: TinyDnsFileSource loads all zone files from a configured directory and feeds lines into the parser, with caching mentioned in metadata.
insights.solution: When modifying file loading/caching behavior, ensure population remains deterministic with respect to the configured directory state and that cache invalidation (if any) does not cause stale records.

insights.title: Source implementations may not resolve name collisions
insights.problem: Multiple sources or inputs can generate records that conflict within the same zone.
insights.root_cause: EnvVarSource metadata explicitly states it relies on the manager to deconflict record name collisions.
insights.solution: Edits should avoid introducing new conflict resolution logic at the source level unless coordinated with the manager’s reconciliation behavior.

key_files.why: Defines the shared Source abstraction, capability flags, and the supports(record) helper that the rest of the system uses to determine compatibility.
Implements a concrete runtime-driven source that injects environment variable values into TXT records and raises specific exceptions on missing input.
Implements TinyDNS ingestion, including symbol mapping and parsing-to-Record translation, plus file-directory loading for bulk import.
Package initializer; typically affects import/export behavior and module discovery only.

construct_relationships: All concrete source classes (EnvVarSource, TinyDnsBaseSource/TinyDnsFileSource) are expected to implement the BaseSource.populate contract and use BaseSource.supports(record) semantics for capability advertisement
TinyDnsBaseSource translates parsed TinyDNS “symbols” via SYMBOL_MAP into octoDNS Record.new invocations that are then consumed by Zone population
EnvVarSource reads environment variable value via _read_variable and then constructs a TXT record that becomes part of the Zone’s desired state during populate
notable_relationships: Upstream: The manager/provider/processor pipeline likely queries Source capabilities (e.g., SUPPORTS and optional flags like Geo/dynamic subnet) and then invokes populate to load records into Zones

Upstream/Downstream: The manager is responsible for deconflicting record name collisions generated by sources (explicitly referenced for EnvVarSource), so source-level edits must align with manager expectations for record identity and insertion semantics

Downstream: Parsed or generated Record objects produced by sources must match the processor’s expectations for record structure (types, names, and any feature-specific fields), especially for TinyDNS symbol mapping
recommendations: Maintain strict compatibility with the BaseSource contract: class-level capability flags and the populate method’s behavior must remain consistent with how the manager orchestrates population

Preserve EnvVarSource’s exception semantics (EnvironmentVariableNotFoundException and any related custom exceptions) and ensure TXT record generation remains stable so error handling and record shapes remain compatible

If modifying TinyDNS parsing or SYMBOL_MAP behavior, treat it as a semantic contract: symbol-to-record-type mapping and the resulting Record.new structure must remain aligned with existing TinyDNS inputs and the downstream Zone/record reconciliation logic
Be mindful of caching/directory reading behavior in TinyDnsFileSource to avoid introducing nondeterminism or stale imports across repeated runs
Avoid shifting responsibilities for resolving record collisions away from the manager; ensure any changes do not alter how/when conflicts are surfaced or handled

tests/config-semis.description: This folder contains YAML fixture data used by the test suite to validate how semicolons are handled in TXT record values within configuration parsing/serialization. The two fixtures establish contrasting scenarios for escaped versus unescaped semicolons, serving as coverage anchors for parser behavior. Changes to parsing/formatting logic that touches TXT values or escaping must remain consistent with the expectations encoded by these fixtures.
key_behaviors: Provides configuration inputs that exercise escaping semantics for semicolons within TXT string values
Enables comparative assertions between escaped and unescaped semicolon handling paths
Acts as a regression guard against losing, transforming, or incorrectly interpreting literal semicolons in TXT values

insights.title: Escaped semicolon preservation
insights.problem: Escaping (e.g., \;) must result in the literal semicolon character being preserved in the effective TXT value.
insights.root_cause: Parsers/serializers may treat semicolons as delimiters or may unescape/escape incorrectly depending on the config grammar.
insights.solution: Ensure the config parsing and any subsequent re-serialization consistently treat escaped semicolons as literal semicolons in TXT records.

insights.title: Unescaped semicolon contrast case
insights.problem: Unescaped semicolons either must be accepted with specific behavior or must be rejected, per the test suite’s expected semantics.
insights.root_cause: Semicolons may be interpreted as separators in certain configuration syntaxes, causing ambiguity without escaping.
insights.solution: Maintain the intended acceptance/rejection behavior for unescaped semicolons so that the contrast fixture continues to reflect the expected rule.

insights.title: Round-trip and normalization stability
insights.problem: TXT value handling may break across stages (YAML loading, config interpretation, internal representation, plan rendering).
insights.root_cause: Different layers may each apply escaping/unescaping or delimiter parsing, leading to cumulative transformations.
insights.solution: When updating any component involved in TXT value processing, verify that semicolon handling is stable end-to-end for both fixtures.

key_files.why: Fixture defining the expected behavior when semicolons in TXT values are escaped; used to validate preservation of literal semicolon characters.
Contrasting fixture for TXT values containing raw semicolons; used to assert the configured acceptance/rejection or interpretation rules.

construct_relationships: Both YAML fixtures represent inputs that feed into the test harness exercising the same TXT parsing/formatting logic, differing only by escape usage in semicolon characters.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: core config parsing/grammar logic that interprets semicolons within TXT values (potentially layered over YAML processing).
Downstream dependency: any formatting/serialization or normalized representation of TXT records that could re-emit semicolons (escaped or literal) after parsing.
recommendations: Preserve the exact fixture content/escaping semantics when modifying any TXT parsing/serialization logic so that the escaped/unescaped distinction remains meaningful.
Run the full relevant test suite for config parsing and any snapshot/plan rendering steps that may include TXT value normalization, ensuring both fixtures behave as expected.

When editing code that interprets semicolons in configuration values, confirm behavior is consistent across all stages: YAML load -> config interpretation -> internal representation -> any output/round-trip formatting.

tests.description: The tests subtree provides unit and fixture-based verification for core octodns behaviors: plan determinism/safety, provider plan rendering formats, and multiple record and processor primitives (TTL clamping, metadata rendering/up-to-date detection, templating, chunked TXT/SPF parsing, NAPTR and SRV record/value correctness). It matters because these tests implicitly encode behavioral contracts that other runtime code must satisfy, including exact error messages, ordering, and output structure for renderers. The YAML fixtures additionally lock in configuration parsing semantics (including edge cases like semicolon escaping and zone alias loops).
key_behaviors: Ensures deterministic Plan output by requiring consistent ordering of Plan changes
Validates Plan safety checks across multiple precedence/threshold rules and mandatory record constraints
Verifies plan renderer plugins produce exact output structures across multiple formats (logger/JSON/Markdown/HTML) including empty/edge cases
Confirms processor behaviors at both source-zone transformation time and plan filtering time (including cases where changes are suppressed entirely)
Locks templating substitution semantics, including trailing dot handling, context injection, supported value-type limitations, and deterministic parameterization
Asserts record/value parsing, validation, equality/change detection, and templating contracts for specific record types (chunked TXT-like, NAPTR, SRV)
Validates deterministic handling and messaging for configuration loading failures (bad plugin configs, bad imports, schema violations)

insights.title: Plan change ordering is treated as a stable external contract
insights.problem: Non-deterministic ordering would cause renderer output and serialized plan comparisons to vary across runs.
insights.root_cause: Potential reliance on iteration order of internal collections when building Plan.changes.
insights.solution: Maintain deterministic ordering expectations when modifying Plan building logic and any renderer that iterates Plan.changes.

insights.title: Plan safety checks have precedence and gating conditions
insights.problem: Threshold/ratio logic and gating on existing records can produce different allow/deny outcomes depending on which config knobs are applied first.
insights.root_cause: Multiple overlapping safety configuration parameters (e.g., zone thresholds vs global thresholds; min-existing gating).
insights.solution: When altering safety-related computations, preserve precedence and boundary conditions (including required record enforcement like Root NS).

insights.title: Renderer outputs require exact schema/keys and exact formatting
insights.problem: Downstream consumers/tests rely on precise nesting/grouping keys and text formatting (e.g., geo string formatting in Markdown).
insights.root_cause: Renderer logic likely formats values differently depending on empty-plan or group-by behavior.
insights.solution: Any edits impacting Plan serialization/rendering must keep output schema stable (JSON nesting/group keys, Markdown/HTML formatting) for all tested states.

insights.title: Processors are validated at both process_source_zone and process_plan stages
insights.problem: A processor may transform the desired zone but must also filter plan changes according to ownership/identity rules.
insights.root_cause: Two distinct lifecycles—source/target zone processing vs plan-change postprocessing.
insights.solution: When changing processor implementations, ensure both stages remain consistent with test expectations, including suppression semantics (e.g., returning falsey when all changes are filtered).

insights.title: MetaProcessor up-to-date logic depends on specific identity fields (TXT type/name)
insights.problem: Incorrect identity matching can cause plan suppression when changes should occur, or vice versa.
insights.root_cause: Identity-sensitive comparison for meta TXT records (including exact record type and name).
insights.solution: Preserve the identity conditions used to classify meta vs non-meta changes and only suppress plans when the correct meta record is the sole change.

insights.title: Templating semantics include trailing dot normalization and deterministic template invocation parameters
insights.problem: Changes to trailing dot handling or context/call signatures can break template variable substitution or alter error messages/behavior.
insights.root_cause: Templating likely normalizes FQDN/dotted forms and passes structured parameters into template evaluation.
insights.solution: Maintain stable semantics for trailing dot disabled/enabled modes, context callable invocation (desired, provider), and skip behavior for unsupported value types.

insights.title: Record parsers and validators require exact reason strings and escaping rules
insights.problem: Tests assert specific reason strings for validation failures (e.g., semicolon escaping/ASCII validation for chunked values; token counts for SRV).
insights.root_cause: Validation error reporting depends on precise branching and message composition.
insights.solution: When modifying parsing/validation logic, preserve exact boundary behavior and exact textual messages used for failures.

insights.title: YAML fixtures encode non-trivial parsing/serialization behaviors
insights.problem: Semicolon escaping in TXT values, include/merge ordering, and alias loop resolution can be broken by even small changes in YAML loaders or config parsing.
insights.root_cause: YAML parsing and custom serialization likely implement special escaping and ordering rules.
insights.solution: Any changes touching YAML safe_load/safe_dump, config parsing, or zone alias resolution must respect the exact fixture-driven semantics (escaped vs unescaped semicolons; deterministic include/merge behaviors; cycle detection).

key_files.why: Central contract tests for Plan determinism, safety checks, and renderer output formats/schema.
Locks TTL clamping boundaries and invalid processor configuration error behavior.
Defines expected MetaProcessor rendering ordering and identity-sensitive up-to-date detection/suppression rules.
Asserts end-to-end templating substitution semantics, trailing dot behavior, context invocation, and skip rules.
Fixture for zone alias cycle detection; changes to alias resolution must avoid infinite recursion and preserve correct cycle handling.

construct_relationships: Plan safety checks (raise_if_unsafe) are validated indirectly through Plan objects composed from shared Record/Change primitives
Plan renderer output tests depend on Plan.changes determinism and on consistent Plan serialization grouping/nesting logic
Processor tests depend on Zone/Record construction via Record.new and on processor stage semantics (process_source_zone vs process_plan)
Templating processor tests depend on record/value template implementations providing identity/no-op semantics and correct parameter passing
Record-type unit tests (NAPTR, SRV, chunked TXT/SPF helpers) depend on the generic Record factory for validation, and on change-detection logic via Record.changes
notable_relationships: tests/config and tests/config-semis function as cross-cutting fixtures that constrain YAML parsing/serialization and config-driven behaviors used by planning/processing logic
Processor tests (meta/ownership/templating/clamp) likely rely on shared Zone/Record/Change invariants from core octodns modules; changes to those invariants will cascade into multiple test modules
Renderer tests likely depend on plan_outputs registration/config schema validated elsewhere (via fixtures like bad-plan-output-*.yaml) and on stable logger/formatting behavior
recommendations: When editing any logic that influences Plan.changes ordering, renderer output, or Plan safety thresholds, treat the unit tests as enforcing stable external contracts (including exact keys/formatting and boundary behavior).

Any modifications to processors must preserve both lifecycle-stage behaviors: source-zone transformation outputs and subsequent plan-change filtering/suppression semantics (including return truthiness when all changes are removed).

Preserve templating semantics precisely: trailing dot handling, template parameterization, context callable invocation signature, and skip behavior for unsupported custom value types must remain consistent with assertions.
If changing parsing/validation for specific record types (chunked TXT/SPF helpers, NAPTR, SRV), keep exact escaping rules and exact validation reason strings to avoid brittle contract breakage.

When adjusting YAML/config loading/serialization or zone alias resolution, maintain the fixture-defined semantics (escaped vs unescaped semicolons, include/merge behavior, alias loop detection and precedence handling such as always-dry-run).
Run the full tests suite (including fixture-driven scenarios) because cross-file dependencies likely cause indirect failures when shared primitives (Zone/Record/Change/YAML loader) are altered.

octodns.description: The octodns folder contains the core runtime library for OctoDNS plus the CLI-adjacent orchestration that drives configuration loading, zone ingestion, processor execution, provider planning, and plan rendering/applying. It matters because most correctness constraints (IDNA handling, processor hook semantics, provider capability filtering, and plan safety gating) are enforced across module boundaries. Changes within this subtree therefore require careful coordination of shared data models (Zone/Record/Plan) and cross-cutting flags like lenient mode, templating parameters, and logging/output contracts.
key_behaviors: Loads a YAML configuration via Manager and instantiates configured providers, sources, processors, secret handlers, and plan outputs by dotted class paths
Normalizes zone identities and DNS names using IDNA helpers to ensure deterministic keying and case/punycode-insensitive behavior
Builds desired (and existing) Zone state by populating from sources/providers, then runs processor hooks across distinct lifecycle phases before planning
Plans record-level changes in provider implementations, then applies safety constraints and renders plan output formats that include provider meta
Supports CLI operations that either orchestrate planning/dumping/applying or perform specialized async DNS resolution reporting with concurrency limiting

insights.title: Version metadata backward-compatibility via __VERSION__
insights.problem: External callers may rely on either __version__ or a legacy uppercase constant.
insights.root_cause: Package-level exports preserve historical naming even though the comment suggests future removal.
insights.solution: When editing __init__.py, preserve both constants and ensure any version-bump mechanism updates both consistently to avoid import-time breakage.

insights.title: Logging configuration affects visible output and debug/quiet behavior
insights.problem: CLI users expect consistent logging destinations/levels and predictable suppression under --quiet.
insights.root_cause: Argument parsing sets root logger levels and may bypass built-in handlers when a YAML logging config is provided.
insights.solution: When changing CLI arg/logging behavior, validate handler selection, root level overrides, stdout/stderr targeting, and syslog integration to avoid missing or misattributed log lines.

insights.title: Processor hook phases and lenient mode propagation
insights.problem: Processors mutate desired/target/plan objects at different stages; incorrect phase wiring or lenient propagation can cause runtime crashes or policy enforcement mismatches.
insights.root_cause: Providers and processors rely on specific hook names (process_target_zone, process_source_and_target_zones, process_plan) and sometimes require backward-compatible lenient kwarg handling.
insights.solution: When modifying processor/provider interactions, ensure lenient-mode behavior is consistently passed through both provider pre-processing and processor hooks without breaking older processor signatures.

insights.title: DNS name and record value canonicalization must remain stable
insights.problem: Downstream diffing, ownership detection, templating resolution, and serialization depend on consistent canonical internal representations.
insights.root_cause: Modules implement normalization (IDNA encoding/decoding), templating substitution semantics, and trailing-dot enforcement that preserve value subclasses.
insights.solution: When editing processors/utilities affecting names/values, ensure canonicalization preserves expected object/value types and deterministic ordering so plan comparisons remain correct.

insights.title: Ownership/meta processors gate what plans allow to execute
insights.problem: Incorrect ownership/meta record creation or plan filtering can lead to unauthorized deletions/updates or suppressed legitimate changes.
insights.root_cause: OwnershipProcessor injects sentinel TXT records and filters plan changes based on inferred ownership coverage; MetaProcessor injects meta TXT and may suppress no-op meta-only plans.
insights.solution: When modifying these processors, maintain their sentinel naming/TTL semantics, wildcard handling, and plan-change filtering criteria so provider planning/applying remains aligned with manageability intent.

insights.title: Plan.raise_if_unsafe thresholds and renderer outputs are contract-critical
insights.problem: Plan rendering and safety gating influence both operator trust and automation behavior.
insights.root_cause: Plan computes update/delete percentages using thresholds derived from existing zone settings and may mark root NS changes specially unsafe; renderers include meta sections.
insights.solution: When adjusting plan construction/safety logic or output classes, keep the expected schema and safety behaviors stable, including any output filename/path attribute naming expectations.

insights.title: YAML provider record parsing/serialization must match data model expectations
insights.problem: Small mismatches in parsing (e.g., semicolon escaping) or serialization ordering/default TTL omission can corrupt or drift zone state.
insights.root_cause: YamlProvider dynamically maps YAML into registered record/value types and may apply provider-level defaults and normalization rules before/after apply.
insights.solution: When editing YAML provider behavior, verify correctness for TXT/SPF escaping, multi-source populate semantics, zone splitting behavior, and deterministic serialization order to avoid unintended diffs.

insights.title: DNS report aggregation must handle missing answers safely
insights.problem: Resolver inconsistency reporting can crash or emit invalid output when resolver responses are absent or exception paths return unexpected shapes.
insights.root_cause: Async reporting maps dnspython exceptions and membership checks to sentinel strings/structures; prior logic included cases where missing entries were treated as iterable values.
insights.solution: When editing report logic, ensure all code paths normalize resolver result containers to consistent list/primitive types and handle dnspython exceptions without breaking aggregation/output formatting.

key_files.why: Central orchestration point for configuration loading, processor selection/wiring, executor behavior, and end-to-end zone/provider workflow.
Defines the shared provider planning lifecycle, capability filtering, processor phase execution, lenient-mode/backward-compatible hook handling, and Plan creation.
Implements Plan safety gating and renderer output formats (JSON/Markdown/HTML/log) used by CLI/automation consumers.
Defines processor abstraction, hook semantics, default behaviors, and ProcessorException contract relied on across all processors.
Specialized CLI entry that performs async DNS lookups and aggregates results into CSV/JSON, making it sensitive to concurrency and output stability.

construct_relationships: Manager orchestrates instantiation of processors, secret handlers, and providers from config; it also selects/executes processors during dump/sync workflows
Providers (via BaseProvider) execute processor hooks across provider reconciliation phases and may pass lenient into processor hooks with backward-compatible fallback

Processors (BaseProcessor subclasses) mutate Zone instances during process_source_zone / process_target_zone / process_source_and_target_zones and may filter/transform Plan changes during process_plan
Plan.raise_if_unsafe uses computed change classifications (create/update/delete) and special-cases root NS changes, affecting whether apply proceeds
IDNA helpers are used wherever DNS names are normalized for consistent dictionary keys and record identity comparisons across pipeline stages
notable_relationships: Providers and sources share the Zone/Record model: sources populate Zone instances and providers later treat those records as desired or existing state during planning

Processors are a cross-cutting concern: multiple processor modules enforce conventions (ownership/meta, trailing dots, TTL bounds, templating) that change both desired zone content and plan eligibility

CLI commands depend on Manager/Provider/Plan contracts for configuration loading, naming, and output schema; report.py additionally depends on dnspython async behavior and consistent aggregation output
recommendations: Preserve public/import-level contracts like version constants in octodns/__init__.py to avoid breaking external consumers
When editing Manager/provider/processor interactions, maintain hook phase semantics and ensure lenient-mode and backward-compatible kwarg handling do not break older processor implementations
Maintain canonicalization invariants for DNS names and record/value types (especially IDNA encoding/decoding and value subclass preservation when modifying record fields)
Keep Plan safety gating behavior and rendered output schema stable, including any differences in attribute naming used by renderers (e.g., output filename conventions) and inclusion of provider meta
For CLI changes (args/report), verify logging setup behavior (stdout/stderr/syslog) and ensure async report aggregation normalizes all resolver result paths to consistent output types

docs.description: The docs/ subtree defines the Sphinx documentation build for octoDNS, including both end-user guides and developer API reference pages. Its primary architectural importance is that it imports project metadata (octodns.__version__) and performs repository-local source-link rewriting so that generated API documentation correctly points back to the exact GitHub revision being built. Because this configuration can fail hard or produce incorrect links when Git metadata is unavailable or ambiguous, changes to build configuration or module importability must be treated as release-critical for documentation accuracy.
key_behaviors: Build-time introspection of the octoDNS package to populate release/version metadata in Sphinx
Dynamic resolution of a GitHub source-link base based on detected git ref (not a fixed branch/tag)
Run-time rewriting of Sphinx reference nodes (doctree) to redirect repository-local links to GitHub URLs anchored at the resolved ref
Documentation organization using a mix of hand-written narrative pages and Sphinx autosummary-driven API stubs
API documentation surfaces multiple internal subsystems (CLI commands, manager, processors, providers, sources, secrets, records, Zone) through module-level autosummary entry points
Configuration guidance pages document cross-cutting configuration concepts that mirror runtime behaviors (manager/plan/apply lifecycle, processors, dynamic zone/record models)

insights.title: Git ref resolution failure can break or skew docs source links
insights.problem: The Sphinx configuration includes helper logic to determine the correct git reference for GitHub links; if ref resolution fails, it can raise an error and halt the docs build, and in some failure scenarios it may fall back incorrectly.
insights.root_cause: Documentation source-link URLs are derived from git state at build time, and the behavior depends on availability/clarity of git metadata (e.g., detached HEAD, shallow clones, missing git binary).
insights.solution: Treat the docs build environment as part of the correctness contract: ensure git ref detection inputs are stable and failures are intentional and detectable rather than silently producing wrong repository links.

insights.title: Hardcoded GitHub branch assumptions can drift from the built revision
insights.problem: If any doc content or link rewriting assumes a fixed branch (e.g., main) rather than the resolved build ref, published links can point to code that doesn’t match the documentation being built.
insights.root_cause: Source-link generation uses repo-local prefixes and RTD-controlled aliases; mismatches between RTD “latest/stable” semantics and actual checkout/commit can create drift.
insights.solution: Ensure any repository-local link rewriting logic remains aligned with the published docs routing semantics so that links consistently target the same revision as the docs build.

insights.title: Autosummary stubs create a compatibility surface with internal modules
insights.problem: The docs/api stubs declare module paths and symbol coverage for autosummary generation; changes in internal module structure, import side effects, or naming can break doc generation or remove expected pages.
insights.root_cause: Sphinx autosummary depends on importability and stable module/symbol names at build time.
insights.solution: When adjusting underlying Python modules, preserve import-time behavior and module/symbol identifiers expected by the stubs so generated API pages remain consistent.

key_files.why: Central Sphinx configuration; sets version metadata and implements git-aware GitHub source-link rewriting logic that can affect both build success and correctness of published links.
Root landing page wiring together major documentation sections; changes affect navigation and user-facing structure.
Primary narrative guide describing YAML configuration structure and semantics that likely mirror runtime behavior and constraints.
Documents the end-to-end Zone sync lifecycle and plan/apply phases, which must remain conceptually aligned with the implementation.
Autosummary entry point for CLI command API docs; depends on stable importable CLI entrypoints for documentation generation.

construct_relationships: Sphinx release/version field in docs/conf.py depends on octodns.__version__ being importable with sys.path adjusted to include the repository root

GitHub link generation in docs/conf.py depends on helper functions (_detect_git_ref, _detect_docs_ref) to compute a tree URL base and subsequently on _rewrite_repo_local_links to mutate doctree reference nodes
doctree reference rewriting in docs/conf.py relies on repo-local link prefix matching to decide which reference nodes should be converted to GitHub URLs
Narrative pages in docs/ (e.g., configuration and lifecycle guides) conceptually correspond to runtime flows implemented across octodns components (manager/zone/processors/providers/plans)
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency on repository git metadata: docs build correctness depends on the ability to determine a stable ref and map it to GitHub URLs
Dependency on runtime importability of the octodns Python package during documentation generation (version retrieval and autosummary imports)
Cross-folder coupling between docs/api autosummary entries and the octodns module layout (CLI, manager, processors, providers, sources, secrets, records, Zone)
recommendations: When editing docs/conf.py or documentation build wiring, validate that git-based source-link detection behaves correctly under CI/doc builders (shallow clones, detached HEAD, missing git binary) and does not silently generate incorrect links

Ensure any changes to package importability (e.g., module side effects at import time) are compatible with docs generation, since docs/conf.py and autosummary rely on importing octodns and its submodules
If internal module paths, symbol names, or exported APIs referenced by docs/api autosummary directives change, confirm Sphinx autosummary generation still succeeds and produces the expected page set

Maintain conceptual consistency between narrative lifecycle/configuration docs and the actual runtime semantics (manager/plan/apply, processors, dynamic zone/record configuration) to avoid misleading published guidance

..description: This folder at the repository root primarily contains project documentation configuration and the core Python package modules that implement octoDNS runtime behavior. It matters because both documentation build behavior (versioning and GitHub source links) and runtime orchestration (Manager, providers, processors, plans, and CLI) are defined within these files, establishing cross-cutting contracts used by tests and downstream consumers. Changes in this subtree must preserve logging, lifecycle hook semantics, data model invariants (Zone/Record/Plan), and output/rendering formats.
key_behaviors: Documentation builds dynamically derive release/version metadata from the installed package and rewrite repository-local Sphinx links to GitHub URLs anchored to a detected git revision.
The CLI parses common logging/verbosity options and initializes logging consistently for all commands.

The Manager orchestrates end-to-end sync/dump/compare by loading YAML configuration, instantiating providers/sources/processors, normalizing zone identities using IDNA, and running processors across defined lifecycle phases before planning.
Processors implement phase-specific hooks to transform desired/target zone state and plan data, with optional lenient-mode behavior propagation through providers.
Providers perform populate/normalization, apply provider/processors constraints, compute record-level changes, and produce a Plan with safety gating and multiple renderers.
The report CLI asynchronously queries resolvers with bounded concurrency and aggregates per-record consistency results into structured output formats.
Provider YAML integration loads/splits YAML into Zone/Record structures and applies planned changes back to YAML deterministically, including special handling for record-value escaping/normalization.

insights.title: Git ref resolution is treated as a hard requirement
insights.problem: Docs source-link rewriting depends on detecting an appropriate git ref; failing to resolve it can lead to incorrect GitHub links being generated.
insights.root_cause: Helper logic for detecting/referencing repo-local content is sensitive to missing/ambiguous git state (e.g., missing git binary, detached/ambiguous HEAD).
insights.solution: Preserve the fail-fast behavior (raising RuntimeError) and ensure link rewriting logic continues to anchor URLs to the resolved ref rather than defaulting silently to an unrelated branch.

insights.title: Package exposes both __version__ and __VERSION__
insights.problem: External code may depend on either version constant; removing/changing one could break integrations.
insights.root_cause: Historical compatibility preserved by defining both names with identical values.
insights.solution: Maintain both symbols and their semantics during packaging/version updates.

insights.title: Asynchronous reporting must handle missing DNS answers safely
insights.problem: When a resolver produces no entries for a record/type, output aggregation can attempt operations on None or incorrectly determine membership, causing errors or inconsistent report output.
insights.root_cause: Resolver membership checks and exception-to-sentinel mapping must align with dnspython behaviors for NXDOMAIN/timeouts/no-answer.
insights.solution: Preserve explicit safe defaults for missing answers and keep resolver normalization and concurrency limiting consistent with expected output schema.

insights.title: Lenient mode propagation and hook compatibility are cross-cutting
insights.problem: Processors and providers may accept/ignore lenient kwarg depending on version; unconditionally passing it can break older implementations.
insights.root_cause: Backward compatibility requirement in BaseProvider indicates hook signatures vary.
insights.solution: When editing processor/provider interfaces, retain backward-compatible lenient kwarg handling and ensure consistent lifecycle-phase sequencing.

insights.title: IDNA handling is used for stable zone/record keying
insights.problem: Inconsistent encoding/decoding or casing can produce non-deterministic behavior when matching providers, sources, and plan outputs.
insights.root_cause: Custom IdnaDict stores IDNA-encoded keys to provide case/punycode-insensitive lookup semantics.
insights.solution: Preserve the IDNA encode/decode boundaries (label-by-label) and the dict keying rules to keep plan determinism and lookups stable.

insights.title: Plan safety gating and output renderers are strict contracts
insights.problem: Plan.raise_if_unsafe and multiple renderer implementations enforce thresholds and special-case behavior; altering them can change whether plans apply and how outputs are serialized.
insights.root_cause: Safety logic relies on record counts and percentage thresholds; renderers include provider meta and deterministic ordering expectations.
insights.solution: Maintain thresholds, gating conditions, change sorting determinism, and renderer output structures (logger/json/markdown/html) to avoid breaking tests and users.

insights.category: Value normalization & escaping in YAML provider
insights.title: TXT/SPF semicolon escaping behavior is sensitive to configuration
insights.problem: When escaped_semicolons=false, the YAML provider still transformed the wrong data key, corrupting TXT/SPF values; double-escaped semicolons can also be misinterpreted.
insights.root_cause: Conditional normalization logic for semicolon escaping and chunked TXT/SPF representations is error-prone and data-shape-dependent.
insights.solution: Keep YAML parsing/serialization semantics stable for escaped_semicolons and ensure transformations apply to the correct YAML fields/structures without corrupting chunked TXT/SPF values.

insights.category: Processor correctness on record value absence
insights.title: Filters and validators must handle record.value=None
insights.problem: Value-based allow/reject filtering can crash when record.value is None because it tries to access rdata_text.
insights.root_cause: Assumptions that record.value is present are violated for some record types or malformed/lenient inputs.
insights.solution: Ensure shared filter logic continues to treat missing/empty values defensively to keep filtering deterministic and non-crashing.

insights.title: Special processors rely on sentinel/tag conventions
insights.problem: Ownership, ACME management, and meta processors rely on specific marker values and plan suppression; incorrect equality/key matching can cause unwanted deletes, missed ownership, or meta update mis-detection.
insights.root_cause: Sentinel/tag strings and ownership detection depend on exact formatting and phase timing across desired/existing/plan steps.
insights.solution: Preserve marker formats, TTL conventions where applicable, and up-to-date detection semantics to avoid incorrect planning/apply behavior.

key_files.why: Defines documentation versioning and repository-local GitHub source-link rewriting; affects release-anchored API docs and build-time failures.
Establishes package version metadata used by runtime and documentation release fields.
Central orchestration for configuration loading, provider/source instantiation, processor execution phases, and plan generation.
Defines Plan safety gating plus deterministic plan serialization/renderers for multiple output formats.
Loads and writes DNS records from/to YAML, including escaping/normalization semantics that impact provider correctness.

construct_relationships: Manager loads YAML config, instantiates providers (subclasses of BaseProvider), processors (subclasses of BaseProcessor), and config-driven plan output renderers, then orchestrates zone population and processor hook phases before invoking provider planning to create a Plan.

BaseProvider extends BaseSource behavior for both populating existing state and producing provider-specific plans; it runs processor hooks across target and combined source/target phases and passes lenient mode with backward-compatible kwarg handling.

Processors implement phase-specific hooks from BaseProcessor (e.g., process_source_zone, process_target_zone, process_source_and_target_zones, process_plan) and mutate Zone/Record or influence Plan changes.
Plan is created by providers with a set of change objects; Plan sorts changes deterministically and enforces safety via raise_if_unsafe before renderers output JSON/Markdown/HTML/logger formats.
octodns/idna.py provides encode/decode and IdnaDict keying that Manager uses to normalize zone names for deterministic identification across the pipeline.
notable_relationships: docs/conf.py depends on importability of the octoDNS package and relies on git metadata availability at documentation build time to produce correct GitHub source links.
CLI modules (args.py, report.py) depend on Manager for configuration loading and on runtime zone/provider/processors for correct behavior of report output generation and logging.

octodns/provider/yaml.py must align with processor expectations for Record value types and representations (including escaping/normalization), since processors and plan renderers assume consistent value shapes.
processors that mutate Zone desired/target state (e.g., templating, trailing dots, ownership/acme/meta) affect what providers see during planning, thereby changing Plan changes and safety outcomes.
recommendations: When modifying any runtime orchestration or hook signatures, preserve backward compatibility for processors/providers that may not accept lenient kwarg, as BaseProvider indicates conditional handling is required.

Ensure any changes that affect Plan ordering, safety gating thresholds, or renderer schemas are coordinated with existing unit/integration tests that validate deterministic output and strict serialization structure.
Maintain IDNA normalization boundaries and IdnaDict lookup semantics to avoid breaking deterministic zone/record matching across providers and plan generation.

Preserve YAML provider escaping/normalization semantics for TXT/SPF (including behavior under escaped_semicolons=false and handling of chunked/double-escaped semicolons), since downstream processors and plan outputs depend on correct value shapes.
For docs/conf.py, treat git ref detection and source-link rewriting as release-critical; avoid changing failure modes or fallback behaviors that could silently produce incorrect GitHub URLs.
For CLI argument parsing and logging setup, keep flag behavior and log level interactions consistent to avoid breaking command output expectations and test harnesses that may capture stdout/stderr.
